paragraphs: Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  6-Week Foundation Module  ·  Teacher Guide
Who This Is For
ESL students aged 5–7 years.
Absolute beginners with limited or no knowledge of the English alphabet.
Children preparing to enter Cambridge Global English Year 1.
What Children Will Be Able to Do
By the end of the 6 weeks, most children will be able to:
Recognise and name all 26 uppercase and lowercase letters.
Match each letter to its sound.
Blend simple consonant–vowel–consonant (CVC) words, e.g. c-a-t → cat.
Read a few common sight words (I, the, a, is, see…).
Build a tiny sentence, e.g. “I see a cat.”
Feel confident and happy starting Year 1.
The 6-Week Plan at a Glance
How the Materials Are Organised
Each of the 6 weeks has its own folder containing:
A Week Overview — the objectives, the five daily focuses, and materials to prepare.
Five daily lesson plans (Day 1–Day 5) — each about 45–60 minutes, with warm-up, new content, guided and independent practice, and a wrap-up.
How to Use This Module
Read the Week Overview before you start each week.
Prepare the materials listed in each daily plan (most are low-cost or printable).
Follow the daily structure, but adapt the pace to your children — repeat a day if needed.
Use the differentiation ideas to support children who need more help and to stretch those who are ready.
Assess gently, by watching and encouraging — there are no stressful tests.
How This Prepares Children for Year 1
It builds the pre-literacy skills the Year 1 reading activities assume.
It front-loads the phonics that appear throughout Year 1.
It gives children their first English words and the confidence to keep going.
When a child finishes Pre-Unit 0, they are ready to begin Year 1, Unit 1: “Welcome to School.” 🌟
tables:
🧭  What this module is
A gentle 6-week bridge that gets true beginners ready for Cambridge Global English Year 1.
For ESL children aged 5–7 with little or no English. It is fully teacher-led (or led by a parent at home).
Focus: letter recognition → letter sounds → blending simple words → first sight words. Enjoyment and confidence come first — mastery comes later.
Week
Focus
What children learn
Week 1
Learn the Alphabet (A–Z)
Recognise and name all letters, upper & lower case.
Week 2
Phonics (A–M)
Match letters a–m to their sounds.
Week 3
Phonics (N–Z)
Match letters n–z to their sounds.
Week 4
Blending Sounds (CVC)
Blend three sounds into simple words.
Week 5
First Sight Words + Practice
Read common words; build tiny sentences.
Week 6
Alphabet & Sounds Review
Consolidate everything; end-of-module check.

💬  Helpful tools
AI tutor: use it to model clear letter names and sounds, and to play simple listening games with the child (an adult holds the device).
iSpring: interactive slides with embedded audio.
Synthesia: a friendly avatar to model sounds consistently.
Canva: colourful letter cards, picture cards, and worksheets.

paragraphs: Day 1: Letters A–E
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today’s lesson, children will begin to:
Meet the idea of the alphabet — that English is written with 26 special letters.
Recognise and name letters A, B, C, D, E in both uppercase (BIG) and lowercase (small) forms.
Match each new letter to a friendly picture (for example, “A is for Apple”).
Trace and begin to form the letter shapes with fingers, air-writing and tactile materials.
Join in the alphabet song, chants and movement with joy.
Remember: today is about recognition and enjoyment, not perfect writing or mastery.
Materials
Letter cards for A, B, C, D, E — one uppercase and one lowercase card per letter (printable).
A large alphabet chart or wall strip showing all 26 letters (printable A–Z poster).
Picture / visual-association cards: Apple, Ball, Cat, Dog, Elephant (printable, or shown on screen).
Simple letter-tracing sheets for the day’s letters (printable; laminate and reuse with a dry-wipe pen to save paper).
Alphabet song audio or video (any free version works).
Tactile materials: clay or playdough; a sand tray or a tray of dry flour/semolina if available.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly in English with a big smile and a wave: “Hello! Good morning!” Encourage them to wave and say hello back.
Play the alphabet song with actions. The goal is enjoyment — children clap, sway and hum along; they do not need to understand every word yet.
Point to the big A–Z chart and run your finger along it, showing that these are all the letters we will get to know together.
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Introduce each new letter with the same gentle, repeatable rhythm so children know what to expect:
Show the uppercase letter card and say the name clearly and slowly: for example, “This is A.” Say it three times, using big mouth movements.
Children echo the letter name back to you two or three times.
Show the picture card and link them: “A is for Apple!” Let children repeat.
Air-trace the letter shape together, saying a simple direction cue as you go (“down, down, across”).
Show the lowercase letter and say: “Big A and small a are the same letter.” Echo it too.
Then repeat this for every letter today, in order:
A — A is for Apple (show BIG A and small a).
B — B is for Ball (show BIG B and small b).
C — C is for Cat (show BIG C and small c).
D — D is for Dog (show BIG D and small d).
E — E is for Elephant (show BIG E and small e).
Keep the pace lively and full of praise. If attention dips, pause for a stretch or a quick clap of the letters.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Do these three short activities together, moving from one to the next before anyone gets restless:
• Letter recognition
Display today’s cards (and the earlier ones, up to E) on the board.
Point to each letter and have the whole class say its name with you.
Play “Point to the letter”: you say a letter name, children point to it on the chart or hold up the matching card. Speed up for fun.
• Letter–picture matching
Lay out the picture cards. Hold up a letter and ask, “Which picture?”
Together, chant the pattern: “A is for Apple”, “B is for Ball”, “C is for Cat”, and so on.
Let volunteers come up and match a letter to its picture, with lots of applause.
• Move like a letter
Children make the letter shapes with their bodies or arms (stand tall for “T”, curl round for “O”).
Air-trace each letter following your model, big and slow, saying the name as you draw.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Children now work quietly at their own pace while you circulate, praise and help:
• Tracing
Children trace the uppercase and lowercase forms of A, B, C, D, E on their sheets.
They colour the matching pictures (Apple, Ball, Cat, Dog, Elephant).
• Make a letter
Shape one or two of today’s letters from clay or playdough.
Trace letters in the sand tray, salt or flour with a fingertip (a favourite — let them do it again and again).
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Quickly review today’s letters (A, B, C, D, E): hold up each card once more and let children name them.
Sing the alphabet song again, focusing on the letters learned so far (A–E).
Give a quick, exciting peek at tomorrow’s letters (F–J) — just show the cards and smile.
Differentiation
Support
Offer gentle hand-over-hand guidance for tracing, then slowly let the child take over.
Focus on fewer letters (perhaps 2–3) for children who need it — recognising a few well beats rushing all five.
Pair a quieter child with the teacher or a buddy for the matching game.
Let children who are not ready to write simply point and say; naming is the goal.
Extension
Introduce the gentle idea that letters make sounds — “A can say /a/” — without drilling it.
Challenge children to find a classroom object that starts with each letter.
Assessment
This is observation, not a test — watch and jot quick notes as children play and work:
Can the child recognise and name today’s letters when shown a card?
Does the child link a letter to its picture (“A is for Apple”)?
Is the child joining in the songs, chants and movement?
Do the tracing sheets show a good attempt at the letter shapes (formation, not perfection)?
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements when you say each letter name — children read your lips.
Repeat, repeat, repeat: young beginners learn a letter by meeting it many happy times, not once.
Keep the very first day especially gentle; some children may be shy or overwhelmed. A smile and a wave go a long way.
Use your hands, face and voice generously. Point, act, sing and smile.
Praise every attempt. At this age, confidence matters far more than being correct.
Remember these are absolute beginners with no prior English — keep sessions short, warm and playful, and stop while it is still fun.

Simple letter-tracing sheets for the day’s letters (printable; laminate and reuse with a dry-wipe pen to save paper).
Alphabet song audio or video (any free version works).
Tactile materials: clay or playdough; a sand tray or a tray of dry flour/semolina if available.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly in English with a big smile and a wave: “Hello! Good morning!” Encourage them to wave and say hello back.
Quick, playful review of letters A–T: hold up flashcards one at a time and let children call out the names. Cheer every try.
Sing the alphabet song together, focusing on the letters known so far (A–T).
Use a happy gesture (“Something new today!”) to show that new letters are coming.
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Introduce each new letter with the same gentle, repeatable rhythm so children know what to expect:
Show the uppercase letter card and say the name clearly and slowly: for example, “This is A.” Say it three times, using big mouth movements.
Children echo the letter name back to you two or three times.
Show the picture card and link them: “U is for Umbrella!” Let children repeat.
Air-trace the letter shape together, saying a simple direction cue as you go (“down, down, across”).
Show the lowercase letter and say: “Big U and small u are the same letter.” Echo it too.
Then repeat this for every letter today, in order:
U — U is for Umbrella (show BIG U and small u).
V — V is for Van (show BIG V and small v).
W — W is for Water (show BIG W and small w).
X — X is for Box (–x) (show BIG X and small x).
Y — Y is for Yo-yo (show BIG Y and small y).
Z — Z is for Zebra (show BIG Z and small z).
Keep the pace lively and full of praise. If attention dips, pause for a stretch or a quick clap of the letters.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Do these three short activities together, moving from one to the next before anyone gets restless:
• Letter recognition
Display today’s cards (and the earlier ones, up to Z) on the board.
Point to each letter and have the whole class say its name with you.
Play “Point to the letter”: you say a letter name, children point to it on the chart or hold up the matching card. Speed up for fun.
• Letter–picture matching
Lay out the picture cards. Hold up a letter and ask, “Which picture?”
Together, chant the pattern: “U is for Umbrella”, “V is for Van”, “W is for Water”, and so on.
Let volunteers come up and match a letter to its picture, with lots of applause.
• Move like a letter
Children make the letter shapes with their bodies or arms (stand tall for “T”, curl round for “O”).
Air-trace each letter following your model, big and slow, saying the name as you draw.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Children now work quietly at their own pace while you circulate, praise and help:
• Tracing
Children trace the uppercase and lowercase forms of U, V, W, X, Y, Z on their sheets.
They colour the matching pictures (Umbrella, Van, Water, Box (–x), Yo-yo, Zebra).
• Make a letter
Shape one or two of today’s letters from clay or playdough.
Trace letters in the sand tray, salt or flour with a fingertip (a favourite — let them do it again and again).
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Quickly review today’s letters (U, V, W, X, Y, Z): hold up each card once more and let children name them.
Sing the complete alphabet song from A to Z together — you did it!
Celebrate finishing all 26 letters: a round of applause, a cheer, or a “letter parade” around the room.
Give a happy peek ahead: “Next week our letters will start to make sounds!”
Differentiation
Support
Offer gentle hand-over-hand guidance for tracing, then slowly let the child take over.
Focus on fewer letters (perhaps 2–3) for children who need it — recognising a few well beats rushing all five.
Pair a quieter child with the teacher or a buddy for the matching game.
Let children who are not ready to write simply point and say; naming is the goal.
Extension
Invite children to begin a simple “My Alphabet Book”, drawing a picture for favourite letters.
Challenge them to name all 26 letters in order, or to find and point out any letter you call.
Assessment
This is observation, not a test — watch and jot quick notes as children play and work:
Can the child recognise and name today’s letters when shown a card?
Does the child link a letter to its picture (“A is for Apple”)?
Is the child joining in the songs, chants and movement?
Do the tracing sheets show a good attempt at the letter shapes (formation, not perfection)?
Do a short, friendly one-to-one alphabet check: show a few letters from across A–Z and note which each child can name.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements when you say each letter name — children read your lips.
Repeat, repeat, repeat: young beginners learn a letter by meeting it many happy times, not once.
Use your hands, face and voice generously. Point, act, sing and smile.
Praise every attempt. At this age, confidence matters far more than being correct.
Remember these are absolute beginners with no prior English — keep sessions short, warm and playful, and stop while it is still fun.
Make today feel like a celebration — the children have met all 26 letters. Name each one’s effort out loud.
tables:
💡  Low-cost & digital options
No printer? Draw the day’s letters BIG on paper or a board — that works just as well.
Tactile writing can use a finger in sand, salt on a tray, or simply a fingertip on the table.
Digital classes can show the letters and pictures on screen using Canva slides or the iSpring interactive deck instead of printed cards.

🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
AI tutor: ask it to run a gentle whole-alphabet game (“Say a letter and I will find it”) to celebrate finishing A–Z.
Synthesia: play the full alphabet song with the avatar so the class can sing along from A to Z.
Canva / iSpring: show the complete A–Z chart on screen for the final sing-along and celebration.

paragraphs: Week 1: Learn the Alphabet (A–Z)
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 1, children will begin to:
Recognise all 26 letters of the English alphabet.
Identify and name letters in both uppercase and lowercase forms.
Trace and begin to form basic letter shapes.
Associate each letter with a simple, friendly picture cue.
Join in alphabet songs, chants and movement with confidence and joy.
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 — Letters A–E: meeting the alphabet. Apple, Ball, Cat, Dog, Elephant.
Day 2 — Letters F–J: review A–E. Fish, Goat, Hat, Igloo, Jam.
Day 3 — Letters K–O: review A–J. Kite, Lion, Moon, Nest, Orange.
Day 4 — Letters P–T: review A–O. Pen, Queen, Rainbow, Sun, Tree.
Day 5 — Letters U–Z: review A–T, then celebrate the whole alphabet! Umbrella, Van, Water, Box (–x), Yo-yo, Zebra.
Materials to Prepare
A large A–Z alphabet chart or wall strip (printable).
Letter flashcards — uppercase and lowercase for all 26 letters.
Picture / visual-association cards for each day’s words.
Letter-tracing sheets (laminate to reuse and save paper).
Alphabet song audio or video.
Tactile materials: clay or playdough, and a sand / salt / flour tray if available.
Optional digital set-up: Canva slides, the iSpring interactive deck, Synthesia avatar clips, and the AI tutor for modelling letter names.
A Note on Assessment
Assessment this week is warm and observational — there is no formal test. As children sing, point, match and trace, notice:
Which letters each child can recognise and name.
Whether they are joining in songs and activities.
Their tracing attempts (looking for effort and formation, not neat perfection).
On Day 5, a short one-to-one alphabet check gives a gentle picture of each child’s progress. Celebrate every step — confidence is the most important outcome of Week 1.
tables:
🧭  Week at a glance
This week the children meet all 26 letters of the English alphabet — both uppercase (BIG) and lowercase (small) — through songs, pictures, movement and play.
Five gentle days, five new sets of letters. Each day reviews the last, so the alphabet grows a little at a time.
The goal is recognition and enjoyment, NOT mastery. Every child’s try is a success. Sounds come next week!

paragraphs: Day 1: Letter Sounds a, b, c
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the names and shapes of a, b, c from Week 1.
Discover the big idea that letters make sounds.
Hear and copy the sounds a (/a/ as in *apple*), b (/b/ as in *ball*) and c (/k/ as in *cat*).
Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.
Link each sound to a fun mouth movement and action.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards for a, b, c (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards: apple, ball, cat.
Simple mouth-shape pictures for /a/, /b/, /k/.
A basket or box of small objects/pictures starting with these sounds.
Sound-matching / colouring worksheet.
Small mirrors if available.
Digital options
Recorded clips of /a/, /b/, /k/ for clear, repeatable modelling.
AI tutor on a screen to say each sound and show the mouth shape.
A three-slide Canva set: one big letter + picture + action per slide.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile and a simple wave; settle them in a circle.
Hold up letter cards a, b, c and ask, “What is this letter?” — celebrate their Week 1 knowledge.
Sing a short, familiar alphabet song, slowing down on a, b, c.
Whisper like it is a secret: “Today we learn something new — letters can make little sounds!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Hold up a. Say: “This is a. a makes the sound /a/.” Open your mouth wide and let them see.
Show the apple picture: “a says /a/ — /a/ apple.” Everyone copies: /a/ /a/ /a/.
Hold up b. Say: “b makes the sound /b/.” Show your lips popping together, then apart.
Show the ball picture: “b says /b/ — /b/ ball.” Children bounce a pretend ball as they say it.
Hold up c. Say: “c makes the sound /k/.” Feel the sound at the back of the mouth.
Show the cat picture: “c says /k/ — /k/ cat.” Children make soft cat paws.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound recognition:
You make a sound; children point to the matching letter card.
You show a picture (apple/ball/cat); children say its beginning sound together.
Mouth-watching:
Children use mirrors (or watch a partner) to see their mouth for /a/, /b/, /k/.
Practise gently switching between the three sounds.
Move to the sound:
/a/ — open arms wide like a big apple tree.
/b/ — bounce hands like a ball.
/k/ — make soft cat paws.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound-matching worksheet: draw a line from each letter to the picture that starts with its sound.
Colour only the pictures that begin with /a/, /b/ or /k/.
Sorting: place objects/picture cards into the correct a, b or c basket.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Recap the three sounds with their actions.
Play a quick game: “Show me the letter that says /b/!”
Give a peek at tomorrow: “Tomorrow we meet the sounds d, e, f!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on just one or two sounds; give lots of extra pictures, actions and gentle repetition.
Sit unsure children near you so they can watch your mouth closely.
Let a child simply copy the action if the sound is still tricky — that still counts.
Extension:
Ask children to find one more thing in the room that starts with /a/, /b/ or /k/.
Gently mention that some letters can make more than one sound in different words.
Assessment
Notice who can copy each sound clearly.
Note who can point to the right letter when they hear a sound.
Glance at the worksheets to see who is linking sounds to letters.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face clearly when you model a sound.
This is about hearing and saying sounds — not writing or spelling. Keep pencils away for most of the lesson.
Keep each activity short and lively; switch before anyone loses interest. Movement resets attention.
Praise effort and brave voices, not just correct answers — warmth keeps shy children trying.
Say the pure sound (“mmm”) not the letter name or “muh” — crisp sounds make reading easier later.
tables:
🖥️  Digital tools & AI tutor
Let the AI tutor model /a/, /b/, /k/ aloud so every child hears the exact same clear sound; children copy the voice.
Build a short iSpring or Synthesia clip of a friendly presenter saying each sound with its action — replay it as a calm brain-break.
Make three bright Canva slides (one big letter + apple/ball/cat + the action) to project during New Content.

paragraphs: Day 2: Letter Sounds d, e, f
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Cheerfully review the sounds a, b, c with their actions.
Hear and copy the sounds d (/d/ as in *dog*), e (/e/ as in *egg*) and f (/f/ as in *fish*).
Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.
Notice how the mouth changes for each sound.
Start to tell one sound apart from another.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards for a–f (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards: dog, egg, fish.
Mouth-shape pictures for /d/, /e/, /f/.
Sound-matching / circling worksheet.
Objects or pictures starting with d, e, f; sorting baskets.
Small mirrors if available.
Digital options
Recorded clips of /d/, /e/, /f/ for consistent modelling.
AI tutor to say the sounds and show the mouth shapes.
Canva review slide of yesterday's a, b, c pictures and actions.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children and quickly review a, b, c using the cards and the actions from Day 1.
Play a fast sound game: you say /a/, /b/ or /k/; children do the matching action.
Signal happily: “We know three sounds — today we add three more!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Hold up d: “d makes the sound /d/.” Tap tongue behind top teeth so they can see.
Show the dog picture: “d says /d/ — /d/ dog.” Children pat a pretend dog.
Hold up e: “e makes the sound /e/.” Show the little smile-mouth for /e/.
Show the egg picture: “e says /e/ — /e/ egg.” Children mime cracking an egg.
Hold up f: “f makes the sound /f/.” Show top teeth resting on the bottom lip.
Show the fish picture: “f says /f/ — /f/ fish.” Children wiggle a hand like a swimming fish.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound recognition:
You make a sound; children point to the matching letter.
You show a picture; children say its beginning sound.
Mouth-watching:
Use mirrors or a partner to watch the mouth for /d/, /e/, /f/.
Practise switching gently between the three sounds.
Move to the sound:
/d/ — pat a pretend dog.
/e/ — crack an egg motion.
/f/ — swim a hand like a fish.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound-matching worksheet: match letters d, e, f to their pictures.
Circle the pictures that begin with /d/, /e/ or /f/.
Sort picture cards into the correct beginning-sound basket.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Review all sounds so far (a–f) quickly with actions.
Play a short “Which letter says…?” game.
Preview: “Tomorrow — g, h, i!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on fewer sounds; add extra pictures, actions and repetition.
Pair a shy child with a confident buddy for the mouth-watching.
Extension:
Hunt around the classroom for extra objects starting with today's sounds.
Invite children to think of a friend or food that starts with /d/, /e/ or /f/.
Assessment
Observe who produces each sound correctly.
Note who matches sounds to the right letters.
Check the worksheets for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face clearly when you model a sound.
This is about hearing and saying sounds — not writing or spelling. Keep pencils away for most of the lesson.
Keep each activity short and lively; switch before anyone loses interest. Movement resets attention.
Praise effort and brave voices, not just correct answers — warmth keeps shy children trying.
Say the pure sound (“mmm”) not the letter name or “muh” — crisp sounds make reading easier later.
tables:
🖥️  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to play a gentle “which sound did you hear?” listening game with /d/, /e/, /f/.
Use a short Synthesia presenter clip to re-model any sound children find tricky, at their own pace.
Keep a running Canva “sound wall” slide, adding d, e, f beside a, b, c so children see the collection grow.

paragraphs: Day 3: Letter Sounds g, h, i
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Review the sounds a–f with their actions.
Hear and copy the sounds g (/g/ as in *goat*), h (/h/ as in *hat*) and i (/i/ as in *igloo*).
Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.
Link each sound to a mouth movement and action.
Grow the skill of telling sounds apart (sound discrimination).
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards for a–i (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards: goat, hat, igloo.
Mouth-shape pictures for /g/, /h/, /i/.
Sound-matching worksheet; “which sound is different?” cards.
Objects or pictures starting with g, h, i; sorting baskets.
Small mirrors if available.
Digital options
Recorded clips of /g/, /h/, /i/ for consistent modelling.
AI tutor to say the sounds and run a listening game.
Canva slides adding g, h, i to the growing sound wall.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children and review a–f using cards and actions.
Play a quick sound game with the sounds learned so far.
Signal: “Three more new sounds today — g, h, i!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Hold up g: “g makes the sound /g/.” Feel it at the back of the mouth (like /k/ but with the voice on).
Show the goat picture: “g says /g/ — /g/ goat.” Children nod their heads like a goat.
Hold up h: “h makes the sound /h/.” Show the soft, breathy puff of air.
Show the hat picture: “h says /h/ — /h/ hat.” Children pop a pretend hat on their head.
Hold up i: “i makes the sound /i/.” Show the little smile-mouth for /i/.
Show the igloo picture: “i says /i/ — /i/ igloo.” Children give a tiny cold shiver.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound recognition:
You make a sound; children point to the matching letter.
You show a picture; children say its beginning sound.
Mouth-watching:
Use mirrors or a partner to watch the mouth for /g/, /h/, /i/.
Feel the breath of /h/ with a hand in front of the mouth.
Practise switching between the three sounds.
Move to the sound:
/g/ — nod like a goat.
/h/ — fan a hand near the mouth to feel the breath.
/i/ — shiver like it is cold inside an igloo.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound-matching worksheet: match letters g, h, i to their pictures.
Colour the pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Sound discrimination: listen to three sounds and clap for the one that is different.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Review all sounds so far (a–i) with actions.
Play a quick sound-spotting game.
Preview: “Tomorrow — j, k, l!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on fewer sounds; give extra visual and touch cues.
For discrimination, start with sounds that are very different (e.g. /g/ vs /i/) before close ones.
Extension:
Let children make a tiny sound booklet, drawing one picture for each new sound.
Invite them to think of another word that starts with /g/, /h/ or /i/.
Assessment
Observe who produces each sound correctly.
Note who matches sounds to letters.
Check the discrimination activity — who can hear the odd one out?
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face clearly when you model a sound.
This is about hearing and saying sounds — not writing or spelling. Keep pencils away for most of the lesson.
Keep each activity short and lively; switch before anyone loses interest. Movement resets attention.
Praise effort and brave voices, not just correct answers — warmth keeps shy children trying.
Say the pure sound (“mmm”) not the letter name or “muh” — crisp sounds make reading easier later.
tables:
🖥️  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to run an “odd-one-out” listening game so children practise telling /g/, /h/, /i/ apart.
Use a Synthesia or iSpring clip to show the breathy /h/ up close — children love feeling the puff of air.
Add g, h, i to the Canva sound wall so the class can see they now know nine sounds.

paragraphs: Day 4: Letter Sounds j, k, l
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Review the sounds a–i with their actions.
Hear and copy the sounds j (/j/ as in *jam*), k (/k/ as in *kite*) and l (/l/ as in *lion*).
Notice that k and c make the same /k/ sound.
Begin to hear these sounds at the start of words.
Enjoy simple sorting and beginning-sound games.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards for a–l (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards: jam, kite, lion.
Mouth-shape pictures for /j/, /k/, /l/.
Sound-matching / circling worksheet.
Objects or pictures starting with j, k, l; sorting baskets.
Small mirrors if available.
Digital options
Recorded clips of /j/, /k/, /l/ for consistent modelling.
AI tutor to lead a beginning-sound sorting game.
Canva slides adding j, k, l to the sound wall.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children and review a–i using cards and actions.
Play a quick sound game with the sounds learned so far.
Signal: “Three more new sounds today — j, k, l!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Hold up j: “j makes the sound /j/.” Show lips pushed forward a little.
Show the jam picture: “j says /j/ — /j/ jam.” Children spread pretend jam on toast.
Hold up k: “k makes the sound /k/” — back of the mouth. Smile: “k sounds just like c! They are sound friends.”
Show the kite picture: “k says /k/ — /k/ kite.” Children fly a pretend kite.
Hold up l: “l makes the sound /l/.” Show the tongue tip up behind the top teeth.
Show the lion picture: “l says /l/ — /l/ lion.” Children roar softly like a lion.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound recognition:
You make a sound; children point to the matching letter.
You show a picture; children say its beginning sound.
Mouth-watching:
Use mirrors or a partner to watch the mouth for /j/, /k/, /l/.
Show that /k/ and /c/ look and feel the same in the mouth.
Practise switching between the three sounds.
Move to the sound:
/j/ — spread pretend jam.
/k/ — fly a kite.
/l/ — roar softly like a lion.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound-matching worksheet: match letters j, k, l to their pictures.
Circle the pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Sorting game: post picture cards into the correct beginning-sound box.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Review all sounds so far (a–l) with actions.
Play a quick sound game; remind them k and c are sound friends.
Preview: “Tomorrow — our last new sound, m, and a big fun review!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on fewer sounds; give extra visual and touch cues.
If /j/ is tricky, let children simply do the action while listening to you say it.
Extension:
Find classroom objects that start with each of today's sounds.
Play “sound friends”: can children find both c and k pictures for the /k/ sound?
Assessment
Observe who produces each sound correctly.
Note who matches sounds to letters, including the shared /k/.
Check the sorting game and worksheets for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face clearly when you model a sound.
This is about hearing and saying sounds — not writing or spelling. Keep pencils away for most of the lesson.
Keep each activity short and lively; switch before anyone loses interest. Movement resets attention.
Praise effort and brave voices, not just correct answers — warmth keeps shy children trying.
Say the pure sound (“mmm”) not the letter name or “muh” — crisp sounds make reading easier later.
Gently point out that k and c make the same /k/ sound — this is a first taste of how English spelling works.
tables:
🖥️  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to host a beginning-sound sorting game — “does jam start with /j/ or /l/?”
Use an iSpring drag-and-drop slide so children sort pictures into the right sound box on screen.
Add j, k, l to the Canva sound wall — the class can now see twelve sounds they know.

paragraphs: Day 5: Letter Sound m and Review
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Review all the sounds a–l with their actions.
Hear and copy the sound m (/m/ as in *moon*).
Practise and enjoy all the sounds a–m together.
Grow the big idea that letters stand for sounds in words.
Gently check who knows which sounds, to guide next week.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards for a–m (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture card: moon (plus all the week's picture cards).
Mouth-shape pictures for the sounds.
Sound-review worksheet; bingo boards and picture cards.
Objects or pictures for all sounds a–m; sorting baskets.
A simple checklist for the one-to-one sound check.
Digital options
Recorded clip of /m/ and a full a–m sound playlist for the review.
AI tutor to run a fun “I Spy the sound” or bingo-style game.
The completed Canva sound wall (a–m) to celebrate on screen.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children and review a–l using cards and actions.
Play a lively sound game with the sounds learned so far.
Signal happily: “One last new sound today — then we celebrate all of them!”
2. New Content (10 min)
Hold up m: “m makes the sound /m/.” Show lips gently pressed together, humming.
Show the moon picture: “m says /m/ — /m/ moon.”
Move to the sound: /m/ — rub your tummy and hum “mmm” like enjoying tasty food.
Let the children hum /m/ together — they can feel their lips tickle.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound review:
You make a sound; children point to the correct letter.
You show a letter; children make its sound with the action.
Sound sorting:
Group sounds by how the mouth looks (lips together: /b/, /m/; teeth on lip: /f/).
Practise moving smoothly from one sound to another.
Sound games:
Play “I Spy” with beginning sounds: “I spy something that starts with /m/…”
Play sound bingo with the picture cards — call a sound, cover the matching picture.
4. Independent Practice (15 min)
Sound-review worksheet: match all the letters a–m to pictures that start with their sounds.
Colour the pictures for sounds the child feels sure about.
One-to-one check (while others work/play): show a letter → child says the sound; say a sound → child points to the letter. Tick the checklist.
5. Wrap-up (5–10 min)
Review all the sounds this week (a–m) with actions, all together.
Celebrate loudly — “We know the sounds for half of the alphabet! Well done!” (a clap, a cheer, a sticker).
Preview: “Next week we learn the rest of the sounds — n all the way to z!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus the check on the sounds each child finds hardest; give extra visual and touch cues.
Let a nervous child do the one-to-one check as a quiet game with you, not a test.
Extension:
Begin exploring how two sounds join: /m/ + /a/ → “ma”; /a/ + /m/ → the start of “am”.
Invite children to blend two known sounds aloud, just for fun.
Assessment
Observe who can produce all the sounds a–m.
Note who matches sounds to letters both ways.
Record each child's progress on the simple checklist.
List the sounds that need more practice in Week 3.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face clearly when you model a sound.
This is about hearing and saying sounds — not writing or spelling. Keep pencils away for most of the lesson.
Keep each activity short and lively; switch before anyone loses interest. Movement resets attention.
Praise effort and brave voices, not just correct answers — warmth keeps shy children trying.
Say the pure sound (“mmm”) not the letter name or “muh” — crisp sounds make reading easier later.
Keep the one-to-one check warm and playful — a smile and a “well tried!” matter more than a perfect score.
Note which sounds were hardest across the class so Week 3 can revisit them.
tables:
🖥️  Digital tools & AI tutor
Let the AI tutor play “I Spy the sound” or a bingo-style review game so children practise all a–m joyfully.
Use the AI tutor for the one-to-one check support: it can say a sound and let a child point, freeing you to observe.
Show the finished Canva sound wall (a–m) on screen and cheer — a Synthesia “well done!” clip makes a lovely close.

paragraphs: Week 2: Phonics (Letter Sounds a–m)
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Guide
Why sounds this week?

In Week 1 the children met the letters as shapes and names (“This is A, this is B”). This week we add the next layer: every letter also makes a sound. Hearing and copying these sounds is the very first step towards reading. Keep the mood light and warm — for a five- to seven-year-old, joyfully copying /m/ while rubbing their tummy is a real success, even if they cannot yet name the letter every time.
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 2, children will be able to (with support):
Hear and copy the sounds for letters a–m.
Begin to match a letter to the sound it makes, and a sound back to its letter.
Notice the first sound in simple, familiar words (e.g. hear the /m/ at the start of “moon”).
Tell some sounds apart from one another when they are said aloud.
Understand the big idea that letters stand for sounds in the words we say and read.
Daily Sound Focus
Day 1 — a, b, c: /a/ as in *apple*, /b/ as in *ball*, /k/ as in *cat*. Introducing the idea that letters make sounds.
Day 2 — d, e, f: /d/ as in *dog*, /e/ as in *egg*, /f/ as in *fish*. Noticing the mouth and beginning sounds.
Day 3 — g, h, i: /g/ as in *goat*, /h/ as in *hat*, /i/ as in *igloo*. Telling sounds apart (discrimination).
Day 4 — j, k, l: /j/ as in *jam*, /k/ as in *kite*, /l/ as in *lion*. Sorting and beginning-sound games (k and c share /k/).
Day 5 — m and review: /m/ as in *moon*, then a friendly review and check of all sounds a–m.
Materials for the Week
Low-cost and printable:
Letter cards a–m (uppercase and lowercase) — print, or write large on card/paper.
Sound-picture cards: apple, ball, cat, dog, egg, fish, goat, hat, igloo, jam, kite, lion, moon.
Simple mouth-shape pictures (open mouth, lips together, teeth on lip) drawn on the board or on cards.
Sound-matching and colouring worksheets; beginning-sound sorting cards or baskets/boxes.
Real objects from around the room or home that start with each sound (a ball, a hat, a cup).
A small mirror per child or per pair if possible, so children can watch their own mouths.
Digital options:
Short audio clips of each letter sound (recorded by you or your AI tutor) for consistent modelling.
A screen or tablet to show the AI tutor saying and animating the sounds.
Slides made in Canva with one big letter, its picture and its action on each slide.
Assessment This Week
Watch and listen daily: can the child copy the sound? Point to the right letter when they hear a sound?
Use the simple sound-matching worksheets to see who is connecting letters and sounds.
On Day 5, do a gentle one-to-one check of sounds a–m and note which sounds to revisit in Week 3.
Keep it stress-free — observe during play and games rather than in a formal “test”.
tables:
🗓️  Week at a glance
Focus: the SOUNDS letters make (a–m), building on the letter shapes and names from Week 1.
Big idea for the children: “Letters are not just shapes — each one has a little sound of its own.”
Everything is teacher-led, multi-sensory and playful: look, listen, say, move. Recognition and enjoyment come first — mastery will come with time.
New sounds are introduced 2–3 per day and gently reviewed the next day, so nothing is rushed.

paragraphs: Day 1: Sounds n, o, p
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the sounds a–m learned in Week 2.
Learn the sounds n (/n/), o (/ɒ/ as in orange), and p (/p/).
Hear these sounds at the beginning of simple words.
Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
Begin to tell apart the close sounds /p/ and /b/.
Materials
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards for today: nest, orange, pen (plus a few more n / o / p pictures).
A small mirror per pair if possible, or the teacher's clearly-visible mouth, for mouth-shape practice.
Sound-matching / sorting worksheet (printable) and crayons.
A few real objects or pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Digital (optional): audio clips of the sounds, an AI tutor for pronunciation, a screen for a short phonics song.
Daily Structure
Total lesson time is about 45–60 minutes, in short and lively chunks. Keep every activity brief, playful, and full of movement.
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile, a wave, and a cheerful “Good morning, everybody!”
Quick sound review of a–m using flashcards and the hand-actions from earlier lessons. Wiggle like a snake for /s/, buzz like a bee for the sounds they know.
Play a fast “make the sound” game: hold up a letter, children say its sound together.
Build excitement: “Today we learn three new sounds: n, o and p!” Use gestures so meaning is clear.
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Introduce one sound at a time. For each: show the letter, say the sound, show your mouth, show the picture, let the children try, then add the action.
Letter n — /n/. “This is n. n says /n/.” Tongue behind the top teeth, a little hum. “/n/ as in nest.” Action: cup hands like a bird's nest.
Letter o — /ɒ/. “This is o. o says /ɒ/.” Open, round mouth. “/ɒ/ as in orange.” Action: make a big round O with mouth and arms.
Letter p — /p/. “This is p. p says /p/.” Lips press then POP with a puff of air. “/p/ as in pen.” Action: pretend to pop a balloon.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound & point: teacher says a sound, children point to the correct letter card. Then show a picture, children say its beginning sound.
Mouth mirror: in pairs, children watch their own mouths (or the teacher's) make /n/, /ɒ/, /p/ and swap between them.
Action chain: call a sound, everyone does its action — nest hands, round O, balloon pop — faster and faster.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Matching worksheet: draw a line from n, o, p to the picture that starts with each sound; colour the pictures.
Sound sort: children sort picture cards into three baskets or hoops labelled n, o, p.
p or b? listen to word pairs and put a hand up for /p/ — a gentle discrimination game.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Recap today's three sounds with their actions.
Play one quick round of “I make the sound, you show the letter.”
Peek ahead: “Tomorrow we meet q, r and s — including a queen!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on just one or two sounds; give extra visual and tactile cues (touch the nest picture, feel the puff of air on a hand).
Let the child copy a partner or the teacher before answering alone. Accept an approximate sound — recognition matters more than perfect pronunciation.
Use the child's home language gently to bridge: name the picture in Somali first, then say the English sound.
Extension
Go on a mini hunt for classroom objects beginning with /n/, /ɒ/ or /p/ (pen, pencil, paper).
Ask the child to be a “sound helper” and model a sound for the group.
Assessment
Can the child produce /n/, /ɒ/, /p/ when shown the letter or picture?
Can the child match each sound to its letter?
Note who separates /p/ from /b/ and who needs another turn.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your mouth clearly.
This is about the SOUND, not the letter name and not writing — keep spelling out of it.
Keep activities short and varied; stop each game while children still want more.
Lean on visuals, actions, and real objects. Praise every attempt warmly.
Point out kindly that p and b look and feel similar but p has a bigger puff of air.
Remember o here is the short /ɒ/ sound (as in orange), not the letter name “oh”.
tables:
👂  Listen closely: p and b
/p/ and /b/ use the same lips, but /p/ is a strong puff of air with no voice, and /b/ buzzes.
Hold a small paper strip near your mouth: it flutters for /p/, stays still for /b/. Children love this.

📱  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to say “/n/, /ɒ/, /p/” slowly so children hear a clear model, then all copy together.
Try a quick digital “beginning sound” game or a short n-o-p phonics song on the screen.
Ask the tutor: “Give me three more picture words that start with /p/” for extra sorting cards.

paragraphs: Day 2: Sounds q, r, s
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the sounds a–p (all of Week 2 plus yesterday's n, o, p).
Learn the sounds q (/kw/), r (/r/), and s (/s/).
Hear these sounds at the beginning of simple words.
Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
Notice that q is a special letter that makes two sounds together.
Materials
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards for today: queen, rainbow, sun (plus a few more q / r / s pictures).
A small mirror per pair if possible, or the teacher's clearly-visible mouth, for mouth-shape practice.
Sound-matching / sorting worksheet (printable) and crayons.
A few real objects or pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Digital (optional): audio clips of the sounds, an AI tutor for pronunciation, a screen for a short phonics song.
Daily Structure
Total lesson time is about 45–60 minutes, in short and lively chunks. Keep every activity brief, playful, and full of movement.
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile, a wave, and a cheerful “Good morning, everybody!”
Quick sound review of a–p using flashcards and the hand-actions from earlier lessons. Pop a balloon for /p/, cup a nest for /n/.
Play a fast “make the sound” game: hold up a letter, children say its sound together.
Build excitement: “Today we learn three new sounds: q, r and s!” Use gestures so meaning is clear.
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Introduce one sound at a time: show the letter, say the sound, show your mouth, show the picture, let the children try, then add the action.
Letter q — /kw/. “This is q. q says /kw/.” Lips round then a little /w/. “/kw/ as in queen.” Action: place a pretend crown on your head.
Letter r — /r/. “This is r. r says /r/.” Tongue curls back, a growly sound. “/r/ as in rainbow.” Action: draw a big rainbow arc with both arms.
Letter s — /s/. “This is s. s says /s/.” Teeth together, a long hiss. “/s/ as in sun.” Action: slither a hand like a snake.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound & point: teacher says a sound, children point to the right letter; show a picture, children give its beginning sound.
Mouth mirror: children watch mouths make /kw/, /r/, /s/ and swap between them, feeling the hiss of /s/.
Action chain: call a sound, everyone does the action — crown, rainbow, snake — speeding up for fun.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Matching worksheet: join q, r, s to the picture that begins with each sound; colour them in.
Sound sort: sort picture cards into baskets labelled q, r, s.
Snake hiss hunt: find and say more /s/ words together (sun, sand, sock).
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Recap q, r, s with their actions.
Quick “sound to letter” game.
Peek ahead: “Tomorrow we meet t, u and v — including a tall tree!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on just one or two sounds; give extra visual and tactile cues (touch the nest picture, feel the puff of air on a hand).
Let the child copy a partner or the teacher before answering alone. Accept an approximate sound — recognition matters more than perfect pronunciation.
Use the child's home language gently to bridge: name the picture in Somali first, then say the English sound.
Extension
Explore how q and u are best friends — spot the u after q in queen and quick.
Ask the child to be a “sound helper” and model a sound for the group.
Assessment
Can the child produce /kw/, /r/, /s/ when shown the letter or picture?
Can the child match each sound to its letter?
Note who can hear that q makes a blended /kw/ sound.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your mouth clearly.
This is about the SOUND, not the letter name and not writing — keep spelling out of it.
Keep activities short and varied; stop each game while children still want more.
Lean on visuals, actions, and real objects. Praise every attempt warmly.
Emphasise gently that q's sound /kw/ is two sounds together — this prepares children for blending next week.
Keep /s/ short and hissy, not a full word; a long snake hiss helps children feel it.
tables:
✨  q is special
q almost never travels alone — it nearly always brings a u with it (queen, quick).
Its sound /kw/ is really two sounds squeezed together: /k/ + /w/. Say it slowly, then quickly.

📱  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to model /kw/ slowly, then at normal speed, so children hear the two sounds join.
Use a short digital rainbow or sun song to make /r/ and /s/ stick.
Ask the tutor: “Show me easy words that start with /r/” for extra picture cards.

paragraphs: Day 3: Sounds t, u, v
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the sounds a–s learned so far.
Learn the sounds t (/t/), u (/ʌ/ as in umbrella), and v (/v/).
Hear these sounds at the beginning of simple words.
Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
Begin to tell apart the close sounds /t/ and /d/, and /v/ and /f/.
Materials
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards for today: tree, umbrella, van (plus a few more t / u / v pictures).
A small mirror per pair if possible, or the teacher's clearly-visible mouth, for mouth-shape practice.
Sound-matching / sorting worksheet (printable) and crayons.
A few real objects or pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Digital (optional): audio clips of the sounds, an AI tutor for pronunciation, a screen for a short phonics song.
Daily Structure
Total lesson time is about 45–60 minutes, in short and lively chunks. Keep every activity brief, playful, and full of movement.
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile, a wave, and a cheerful “Good morning, everybody!”
Quick sound review of a–s using flashcards and the hand-actions from earlier lessons. Hiss like a snake for /s/, draw a rainbow for /r/.
Play a fast “make the sound” game: hold up a letter, children say its sound together.
Build excitement: “Today we learn three new sounds: t, u and v!” Use gestures so meaning is clear.
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Introduce one sound at a time: show the letter, say the sound, show your mouth, show the picture, let the children try, then add the action.
Letter t — /t/. “This is t. t says /t/.” Tongue taps behind top teeth, a crisp tap. “/t/ as in tree.” Action: stand tall and still like a tree.
Letter u — /ʌ/. “This is u. u says /ʌ/.” Mouth relaxed and open. “/ʌ/ as in umbrella.” Action: push an umbrella open above your head.
Letter v — /v/. “This is v. v says /v/.” Top teeth on bottom lip, a buzzing sound. “/v/ as in van.” Action: hold and turn a steering wheel.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound & point: teacher says a sound, children point to the right letter; show a picture, children give its beginning sound.
Mouth mirror: children watch mouths make /t/, /ʌ/, /v/ and feel the buzz of /v/ with a hand on the throat.
Action chain: call a sound, everyone does the action — tall tree, open umbrella, drive the van.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Matching worksheet: join t, u, v to the picture that begins with each sound; colour them in.
Sound sort: sort picture cards into baskets labelled t, u, v.
Buzz or blow? listen to /v/ and /f/ pairs and buzz your lips for /v/.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Recap t, u, v with their actions.
Quick “sound to picture” game.
Peek ahead: “Tomorrow we meet w, x and y — including a splash of water!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on just one or two sounds; give extra visual and tactile cues (touch the nest picture, feel the puff of air on a hand).
Let the child copy a partner or the teacher before answering alone. Accept an approximate sound — recognition matters more than perfect pronunciation.
Use the child's home language gently to bridge: name the picture in Somali first, then say the English sound.
Extension
Go on a mini hunt for classroom objects beginning with /t/, /ʌ/ or /v/ (table, tap).
Ask the child to be a “sound helper” and model a sound for the group.
Assessment
Can the child produce /t/, /ʌ/, /v/ when shown the letter or picture?
Can the child match each sound to its letter?
Note who separates /t/ from /d/ and /v/ from /f/.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your mouth clearly.
This is about the SOUND, not the letter name and not writing — keep spelling out of it.
Keep activities short and varied; stop each game while children still want more.
Lean on visuals, actions, and real objects. Praise every attempt warmly.
Point out kindly the closeness of t/d and v/f; a hand on the throat shows which sounds buzz.
Remember u here is the short /ʌ/ sound (as in umbrella), not the letter name “you”.
tables:
👂  Listen closely: t/d and v/f
/t/ and /d/ tap the same place, but /d/ buzzes with voice while /t/ is a quiet tap.
/v/ and /f/ use teeth-on-lip, but /v/ buzzes and /f/ just blows. Feel your throat: it hums for /v/.

📱  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to say “/t/ … /d/” and “/v/ … /f/” so children hear the buzz-vs-quiet difference.
Play a short digital sound-picture matching game for t, u, v.
Ask the tutor: “Give me simple words that start with /t/” to make extra cards.

paragraphs: Day 4: Sounds w, x, y
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the sounds a–v learned so far.
Learn the sounds w (/w/), x (/ks/), and y (/j/).
Hear these sounds in simple words (and notice where x likes to hide).
Link each sound to a mouth shape and a fun hand-action.
Notice that x is a special letter that makes two sounds together.
Materials
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture cards for today: water, box, yo-yo (plus a few more w / x / y pictures).
A small mirror per pair if possible, or the teacher's clearly-visible mouth, for mouth-shape practice.
Sound-matching / sorting worksheet (printable) and crayons.
A few real objects or pictures that begin with today's sounds.
Digital (optional): audio clips of the sounds, an AI tutor for pronunciation, a screen for a short phonics song.
Daily Structure
Total lesson time is about 45–60 minutes, in short and lively chunks. Keep every activity brief, playful, and full of movement.
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile, a wave, and a cheerful “Good morning, everybody!”
Quick sound review of a–v using flashcards and the hand-actions from earlier lessons. Drive the van for /v/, stand tall for /t/.
Play a fast “make the sound” game: hold up a letter, children say its sound together.
Build excitement: “Today we learn three new sounds: w, x and y!” Use gestures so meaning is clear.
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Introduce one sound at a time: show the letter, say the sound, show your mouth, show the picture, let the children try, then add the action.
Letter w — /w/. “This is w. w says /w/.” Lips round and push forward. “/w/ as in water.” Action: wave hands like flowing water.
Letter x — /ks/. “This is x. x says /ks/.” A quick /k/ then /s/. “/ks/ as in box.” Action: cross both arms into an X shape.
Letter y — /j/. “This is y. y says /j/.” Tongue high near the roof of the mouth. “/j/ as in yo-yo.” Action: bounce a yo-yo up and down.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound & point: teacher says a sound, children point to the right letter; show a picture, children give its beginning sound.
Mouth mirror: children watch mouths make /w/, /ks/, /j/ and swap between them.
Action chain: call a sound, everyone does the action — wavy water, arm X, bouncing yo-yo.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Matching worksheet: join w, x, y to the picture that goes with each sound; colour them in.
Sound sort: sort picture cards into baskets labelled w, x, y.
Find the x: point to the x sound at the end of box, fox, six — an ending-sound game.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Recap w, x, y with their actions.
Quick “sound to letter” game.
Peek ahead: “Tomorrow we meet the LAST sound, z, and celebrate all our sounds!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on just one or two sounds; give extra visual and tactile cues (touch the nest picture, feel the puff of air on a hand).
Let the child copy a partner or the teacher before answering alone. Accept an approximate sound — recognition matters more than perfect pronunciation.
Use the child's home language gently to bridge: name the picture in Somali first, then say the English sound.
Extension
Explore how x usually appears at the END of words, not the beginning — collect ending-x words.
Ask the child to be a “sound helper” and model a sound for the group.
Assessment
Can the child produce /w/, /ks/, /j/ when shown the letter or picture?
Can the child match each sound to its letter?
Note who can hear x as a blended /ks/ sound.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your mouth clearly.
This is about the SOUND, not the letter name and not writing — keep spelling out of it.
Keep activities short and varied; stop each game while children still want more.
Lean on visuals, actions, and real objects. Praise every attempt warmly.
Emphasise gently that x makes a combined /ks/ sound and rarely begins English words.
Note that y here is the consonant sound /j/ (as in yo-yo); its other jobs come much later.
tables:
✨  x is special
x makes two sounds together: /k/ + /s/. Say “bo-k-s” slowly and hear them both.
x is shy at the start of words — it usually hides at the END (box, fox, six). We hunt for it there.

📱  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to stretch out “bo-k-s” so children hear the two sounds inside x.
Play a short digital water / yo-yo phonics clip to make /w/ and /j/ memorable.
Ask the tutor: “Give me words that END in the x sound” for the ending-sound hunt.

paragraphs: Day 5: Sound z and Full Review
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
Warmly review the sounds a–y learned so far.
Learn the final sound z (/z/).
Practise ALL of this week's new sounds (n–z).
Join everything up to recognise all 26 letter sounds (a–z).
Take part in a gentle one-to-one sound check and CELEBRATE finishing the alphabet sounds.
Materials
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase).
Sound-picture card for today: zebra (plus a full set of a–z pictures for review).
A small mirror per pair if possible, or the teacher's clearly-visible mouth, for mouth-shape practice.
Sound-matching / sorting worksheet (printable) and crayons.
A few real objects or pictures that begin with today's sounds.
A simple sound-check sheet for quick one-to-one notes (letter shown → sound; sound made → child points).
Digital (optional): audio clips of the sounds, an AI tutor for pronunciation, a screen for a short phonics song.
Daily Structure
Total lesson time is about 45–60 minutes, in short and lively chunks. Keep every activity brief, playful, and full of movement.
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile, a wave, and a cheerful “Good morning, everybody!”
Quick sound review of a–y using flashcards and the hand-actions from earlier lessons. Wave like water for /w/, bounce a yo-yo for /j/.
Play a fast “make the sound” game: hold up a letter, children say its sound together.
Build excitement: “Today we learn the very LAST sound: z!” Use gestures so meaning is clear.
2.  New Content  (10 min)
Only one new sound today, so we can enjoy it and then celebrate the whole alphabet.
Letter z — /z/. “This is z. z says /z/.” Teeth together like /s/, but now it BUZZES. “/z/ as in zebra.” Action: zig-zag a hand like zebra stripes.
Feel the buzz: put a hand on your throat. /s/ is quiet, /z/ hums — they are a snake and a bee.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound review: teacher says a sound → children point to the letter; teacher shows a letter → children make the sound. Roam right across a–z.
Sound families: group sounds by mouth shape — lip-poppers (/p/,/b/), buzzers (/v/,/z/), hissers (/s/), hummers (/n/,/m/).
“I Spy” beginning sounds: “I spy something starting with /s/…” using classroom objects.
Sound bingo: children cover the picture whose beginning sound the teacher says.
4.  Independent Practice  (15 min)
A–Z review worksheet: match letters to pictures that begin with their sounds; colour freely.
One-to-one sound check: quietly, while others work, show each child a few letters to sound out and say a few sounds for them to point to. Note results kindly.
5.  Wrap-up  (5–10 min)
Review the whole alphabet of sounds a–z together, with actions.
Celebrate! A round of applause — “We know ALL the sounds now!” Perhaps a sticker or a happy song.
Peek ahead: “Next week we start joining sounds together to make little words!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus the check on the child's most confident sounds first to build success, then try one or two trickier ones.
Allow pointing and copying; accept close approximations. Recognition and enjoyment come before accuracy.
Bridge with the home language: name the picture in Somali, then say the English sound together.
Extension
Begin blending two sounds into a tiny word aloud, e.g. /s/ + /o/ → “so”, as a playful taste of next week.
Let confident children be “sound leaders” in bingo and I Spy.
Assessment
Observe whether the child can produce sounds across a–z.
Note which sounds are secure and which to revisit before blending week.
Record simple one-to-one results for each child; celebrate progress, not perfection.
Teacher Notes
Use big, exaggerated mouth movements and make sure every child can see your mouth clearly.
This is about the SOUND, not the letter name and not writing — keep spelling out of it.
Keep activities short and varied; stop each game while children still want more.
Lean on visuals, actions, and real objects. Praise every attempt warmly.
Point out kindly that z is the buzzing partner of s — same mouth, voice on.
This is a joyful milestone: the children now know all 26 letter sounds. Make the celebration real.
Use your one-to-one notes to plan gentle review of any wobbly sounds next week.
tables:
🐝  Listen closely: s and z
/s/ and /z/ look the same in the mouth, but /z/ turns the voice on and buzzes.
Play “snake or bee?” — hiss for /s/, buzz for /z/, hands on throats to feel the difference.

📱  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to play a quick a–z sound review game, calling sounds for children to point out.
Use the tutor to model the /s/ vs /z/ buzz clearly for the whole class.
Ask the tutor: “List easy CVC words for next week's blending” to prepare Day-1 of blending.

paragraphs: Week 3: Phonics (N–Z)
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 3, children will be able to (with support and lots of repetition):
Recognise and say the sounds for letters n through z.
Match these letters to their sounds using pictures and actions.
Hear the beginning sound in simple, familiar words.
Tell apart sounds that are easy to mix up, such as /p/ and /b/, /t/ and /d/, /s/ and /z/.
Join up their whole-week learning to know all 26 letter sounds (a–z) in a fun, low-pressure way.
Daily Sound Focus
Day 1  —  Sounds n, o, p
/n/ as in nest, /ɒ/ as in orange, /p/ as in pen.
Notice how /p/ is a strong puff of air, close to /b/ from last week.
Day 2  —  Sounds q, r, s
/kw/ as in queen, /r/ as in rainbow, /s/ as in sun.
Notice how q is special — it makes two sounds together and almost always brings a u with it.
Day 3  —  Sounds t, u, v
/t/ as in tree, /ʌ/ as in umbrella, /v/ as in van.
Notice how /t/ is close to /d/, and /v/ is close to /f/.
Day 4  —  Sounds w, x, y
/w/ as in water, /ks/ as in box, /j/ as in yo-yo.
Notice how x is special — it makes two sounds (/k/ + /s/) and usually hides at the END of words.
Day 5  —  Sound z and full review
/z/ as in zebra, then a happy review of ALL sounds a–z.
Notice how /z/ buzzes in your throat, close to /s/ but noisier.
Materials for the Week
Everything below is low-cost, printable, or a simple classroom object. Digital tools are optional extras.
Letter cards a–z (uppercase and lowercase) — printed or hand-made on card.
Sound-picture cards: nest, orange, pen, queen, rainbow, sun, tree, umbrella, van, water, box, yo-yo, zebra.
Simple mouth-position pictures (or just the teacher's own mouth) for each new sound.
Sound-sorting and matching worksheets (printable), crayons for colouring.
Real objects or pictures from home and class that begin with each sound.
Optional digital: audio recordings of the sounds, an AI tutor to model pronunciation, a projector for phonics songs.
Assessment This Week
Assessment is gentle and by observation — there are no tests and no writing. Watch and note who is confident and who needs another turn.
Daily observation: can the child say the sound and point to the right letter or picture?
Beginning-sound checks during games (“What sound does *sun* start with?”).
A short, friendly one-to-one sound check on Day 5 covering a–z.
Keep simple notes on which sounds to revisit before next week's blending work.
tables:
🗓️  Week at a glance
This week we finish the alphabet SOUNDS, working through the letters n to z. The children already met the sounds a to m in Week 2, so this week completes the full set of 26 letter sounds.
The focus is on hearing and saying each sound (phonics), NOT on writing or spelling. We link every sound to a picture, a hand movement, and a happy little game.
Keep it playful and warm. We want children to recognise and enjoy the sounds, not to master them perfectly. Praise every attempt.

paragraphs: Day 1: Introduction to Sound Blending – pushing sounds together
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today, children will begin to:
Recall the letter sounds learned in Weeks 2 and 3.
Understand the big idea that separate sounds can be pushed together to make a word.
Break a simple spoken word into its separate sounds (segmenting).
Blend the first two sounds of a word - a consonant + a vowel (for example /b/ + /a/ = /ba/).
Materials
Letter cards for all sounds learned so far.
Three-box sound boxes (three squares drawn in a row).
Picture cards of simple objects (cat, sun, map …).
Magnetic letters or letter tiles.
Optional: short audio clips of sounds being segmented and blended; class AI tutor.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a smile and a familiar gesture. A calm, happy start settles everyone.
Flash letter cards quickly and have the class call out each sound together - a fast, playful review.
Play a quick “Sound Hunt”: you make a sound (/m/), children point to the matching letter card.
Use a gesture (hands coming together) to hint: “Today we learn something new and exciting with our sounds!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Introduce blending with one clear, friendly example. Model, do not explain in long words.
Hold up a picture of a cat. Smile: “This is a cat. Let us listen to the sounds hiding inside ‘cat’.”
Segment slowly and clearly, one sound at a time: /k/ … /a/ … /t/. Raise one finger for each sound.
Now blend: “/k/ + /a/ makes /ka/ … then add /t/ … cat!” Sweep your hands together as the word appears.
Place one letter in each of the three sound boxes so children see the three sounds sitting in three homes.
Show how the sounds “push together” by sliding the boxes closer as you blend. Say it again, a little faster.
Repeat the see-hear-blend routine with map and sun so the pattern feels familiar.
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound-by-sound, together. Keep it light and full of praise.
Activity A – Segmenting together:
Say a simple word (“mat”). Children echo the whole word back.
Together, break it into sounds: /m/ … /a/ … /t/, raising a finger per sound.
Add a movement per sound - tap the shoulder, elbow, wrist - so the body feels the three parts.
Activity B – Blending the first two sounds:
Show two letter cards, a consonant and vowel a: /b/ and /a/.
Push the cards together and blend: /b/ + /a/ = /ba/. Children copy the push and the sound.
Swap the consonant: /m/ + /a/ = /ma/, /s/ + /a/ = /sa/, /h/ + /a/ = /ha/. Make it a happy chant.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Children try with your support close by. Two gentle stations or a whole-class turn-taking:
Sound boxes: children place two letter cards (consonant + vowel) in the boxes and push them together to say the blended part.
Picture sounds: show a picture; children say the first two sounds and blend them (“su…” for sun).
Move around, listen, and join in. If a child freezes, model it once and let them copy - no pressure.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Come back together and blend two or three fun examples as a class.
Celebrate: “Today we learned to push sounds together - that is how we read!”
Preview with a smile: “Tomorrow we will read whole words with the /a/ sound, like cat and hat!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus only on hearing the first sound of a word, then just the first two together.
Use fewer examples and give extra thinking time. Let the child watch a peer first.
Extension
Children who grasp it quickly can try blending all three sounds of a short-a word.
Ask them to find the picture that matches a word you segment.
Assessment
Observe: can the child break a spoken word into separate sounds?
Note who is starting to blend the first two sounds together.
Check understanding informally through the picture-sound activity.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements when you segment and blend. Let children watch your lips - seeing the sound helps as much as hearing it.
Show the “push” with your hands: one hand per sound, then sweep them together as you blend. Children love copying this.
Keep the pace slow. Blending is hard mental work for a 5–7 year old, and doubly so in a new language.
Praise the effort and the try, not only the right answer - “Great listening!”, “You pushed those sounds together!”
Say sounds cleanly: /t/ not “tuh”, /m/ not “muh”. A stretched-out extra “uh” makes blending harder.
This is a brand-new concept - expect confusion and keep it playful. Today is about the idea of blending, not mastery.
tables:
🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to sound out a CVC word slowly (“/m/ … /a/ … /p/ … map”) so children hear clean, steady blending.
Use the AI tutor to play a first-sound game: it says a word, children name the sound it starts with.
Let a shy child practise blending quietly with the tutor before trying in front of the group.

paragraphs: Day 2: Blending with Short a – cat, hat, map
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today, children will begin to:
Recall how to push separate sounds together to blend.
Segment CVC words that use the short a sound into three sounds.
Blend three sounds to read whole short-a words such as cat, hat, map, bat, fan.
Match a short-a word to the picture it names.
Materials
Letter cards for all sounds learned.
Three-box sound boxes.
Picture cards for short-a words: cat, hat, map, bat, fan, bag.
Word-building mats and magnetic letters or letter tiles.
CVC word cards with short a.
Optional: audio clips of short-a words; class AI tutor.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a happy “Assalaamu alaykum / hello” smile.
Recall yesterday: “What do we do with our sounds? We push them together!” Do the hand sweep together.
Segment two or three easy words as a class, raising a finger per sound.
Signal the day: “Today we read WHOLE words with the /a/ sound!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Model reading full short-a words. Always: hear the 3 sounds → blend → read the word.
Show the cat picture: “This is a cat. Listen: /k/ … /a/ … /t/.” One finger per sound.
Blend it: “/k/ + /a/ = /ka/ … add /t/ … cat!” Then reveal the written word cat beside the picture.
Repeat the same routine with hat (/h/ /a/ /t/), map (/m/ /a/ /p/), and bat (/b/ /a/ /t/).
Point to the /a/ in the middle each time: “Same sound in the middle - /a/, /a/, /a/.”
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound Blending together:
You segment a word sound by sound; children echo each sound.
Together, blend the sounds into the whole word.
Hold up two pictures; children choose the one that matches the word.
Word Building - change one sound:
Build cat in the sound boxes with letter tiles.
Change one letter at a time and re-read: cat → hat → mat → map.
Cheer each new word - children love seeing one small change make a new word.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound boxes: children place three letters, push them together, and read the word, then find its picture.
Word-picture matching: match written short-a words to pictures; or draw a picture for a word card (cat, bag).
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Read three short-a words together as a class, nice and proud.
Praise: “You just READ words in English - well done!”
Preview: “Tomorrow, new middle sounds - /e/ and /i/, like bed and pin!”
Differentiation
Support
Work with fewer words (just cat and hat) and give extra visual and tactile cues (touch each box).
Let the child blend along right after you model, rather than alone.
Extension
Build a tiny sentence with the words: “The cat sat.”
Ask the child to think of another short-a word and build it.
Assessment
Observe segmenting of short-a words into three sounds.
Note progress blending three sounds into a whole word.
Check word-picture matching for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements when you segment and blend. Let children watch your lips - seeing the sound helps as much as hearing it.
Show the “push” with your hands: one hand per sound, then sweep them together as you blend. Children love copying this.
Keep the pace slow. Blending is hard mental work for a 5–7 year old, and doubly so in a new language.
Praise the effort and the try, not only the right answer - “Great listening!”, “You pushed those sounds together!”
Say sounds cleanly: /t/ not “tuh”, /m/ not “muh”. A stretched-out extra “uh” makes blending harder.
Reading is a brand-new skill arriving alongside a new language - keep expectations gentle and joyful.
tables:
🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to sound out short-a words (“/h/ … /a/ … /t/ … hat”) so children hear steady, clean blending.
Let the tutor play a word-picture matching game: it names a word, the child points to the right picture.
Have the tutor listen to a child blend and offer warm, simple encouragement.

paragraphs: Day 3: Blending with Short e and i – bed, pen, pin, sit
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today, children will begin to:
Recall reading short-a CVC words from yesterday.
Segment and blend CVC words with the short e sound (bed, pen, leg, net).
Segment and blend CVC words with the short i sound (pin, big, hit, sit).
Match these words to pictures and grow more confident reading them.
Materials
Letter cards for all sounds learned.
Three-box sound boxes.
Picture cards for short e (bed, pen, leg, net) and short i (pin, big, hit, sit).
Word-building mats and magnetic letters or letter tiles.
CVC word cards with short e and i.
Optional: audio clips; class AI tutor.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a quick smile-check that everyone is ready.
Review short-a words: read cat, hat, map together, blending aloud.
Do the hand-push movement to remind everyone how blending feels.
Signal the day: “Today, two new middle sounds - /e/ and /i/!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Short e first, then short i. Same routine every time: hear 3 sounds → blend → read.
Short e:
Show bed: “This is a bed. Listen: /b/ … /e/ … /d/.”
Blend: “/b/ + /e/ = /be/ … add /d/ … bed!” Show the written word beside the picture.
Repeat with pen (/p/ /e/ /n/) and leg (/l/ /e/ /g/).
Short i:
Show pin: “This is a pin. Listen: /p/ … /i/ … /n/.”
Blend: “/p/ + /i/ = /pi/ … add /n/ … pin!” Show the written word beside the picture.
Repeat with big (/b/ /i/ /g/) and sit (/s/ /i/ /t/).
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound Blending together:
You segment a word; children echo each sound.
Blend together into the whole word, then match it to a picture.
Word Building - change one sound:
bed → red → fed (keep the /e/ in the middle).
pin → bin → big → dig (feel the /i/ in the middle).
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound boxes: build and read short-e and short-i words, then match to pictures.
Vowel sorting: sort picture cards into groups by their middle sound - a, e, or i.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Read one short-e and one short-i word together, proudly.
Praise the careful listening for the middle sound.
Preview: “Tomorrow, /o/ and /u/ - dog and sun!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on one vowel at a time (just short e today, short i another moment).
Give extra visual and tactile cues; let the child watch, then copy.
Extension
Build a short phrase: “the big pen”.
Challenge the child to sort a mixed pile of a/e/i pictures alone.
Assessment
Observe segmenting into three sounds.
Note blending progress with the new middle sounds.
Watch for confusion between /e/ and /i/ - note who needs more practice.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements when you segment and blend. Let children watch your lips - seeing the sound helps as much as hearing it.
Show the “push” with your hands: one hand per sound, then sweep them together as you blend. Children love copying this.
Keep the pace slow. Blending is hard mental work for a 5–7 year old, and doubly so in a new language.
Praise the effort and the try, not only the right answer - “Great listening!”, “You pushed those sounds together!”
Say sounds cleanly: /t/ not “tuh”, /m/ not “muh”. A stretched-out extra “uh” makes blending harder.
Help children notice the different mouth shapes for /e/ (mouth relaxed, open) and /i/ (small smile). Let them feel their own mouths.
tables:
🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to sound out short-e and short-i words slowly so children can compare the middle sounds.
Use the tutor to play a vowel-sorting game: it says a word, children say if the middle is /e/ or /i/.
Let a child who mixes up /e/ and /i/ practise a few words one-to-one with the tutor.

paragraphs: Day 4: Blending with Short o and u – dog, top, sun, cup
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today, children will begin to:
Recall reading CVC words with short a, e, and i.
Segment and blend CVC words with the short o sound (dog, top, pot, fox).
Segment and blend CVC words with the short u sound (sun, cup, bug, rug).
Match these words to pictures and read them with growing confidence.
Materials
Letter cards for all sounds learned.
Three-box sound boxes.
Picture cards for short o (dog, top, pot, fox) and short u (sun, cup, bug, rug).
Word-building mats and magnetic letters or letter tiles.
