CVC word cards with short o and u.
Optional: audio clips; class AI tutor.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a happy, settled start.
Review a, e, i: read one word with each vowel together (cat, bed, pin).
Do the hand-push blending movement as a group.
Signal the day: “The last two middle sounds today - /o/ and /u/!”
2. New Content (10–15 min)
Short o first, then short u. Same routine: hear 3 sounds → blend → read.
Short o:
Show dog: “This is a dog. Listen: /d/ … /o/ … /g/.”
Blend: “/d/ + /o/ = /do/ … add /g/ … dog!” Show the written word beside the picture.
Repeat with top (/t/ /o/ /p/) and pot (/p/ /o/ /t/).
Short u:
Show sun: “This is the sun. Listen: /s/ … /u/ … /n/.”
Blend: “/s/ + /u/ = /su/ … add /n/ … sun!” Show the written word beside the picture.
Repeat with cup (/k/ /u/ /p/) and bug (/b/ /u/ /g/).
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Sound Blending together:
You segment a word; children echo each sound.
Blend together into the whole word, then match it to a picture.
Word Building - change one sound:
dog → log → lot → pot.
sun → run → rug → bug.
4. Independent Practice (10–15 min)
Sound boxes: build and read short-o and short-u words, then match to pictures.
Vowel sorting: now sort pictures across all five vowels - a, e, i, o, u.
5. Wrap-up (5 min)
Read one short-o and one short-u word together.
Celebrate: “We now know ALL five middle sounds!”
Preview: “Tomorrow we mix them all up and play reading games!”
Differentiation
Support
Focus on one vowel at a time; give extra visual and tactile cues.
Keep to two or three words so success feels reachable.
Extension
Build a playful phrase: “the dog and the cup”.
Challenge the child to sort all five vowel groups independently.
Assessment
Observe segmenting into three sounds.
Note blending progress with /o/ and /u/.
Watch for vowel confusion and note who needs review.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements when you segment and blend. Let children watch your lips - seeing the sound helps as much as hearing it.
Show the “push” with your hands: one hand per sound, then sweep them together as you blend. Children love copying this.
Keep the pace slow. Blending is hard mental work for a 5–7 year old, and doubly so in a new language.
Praise the effort and the try, not only the right answer - “Great listening!”, “You pushed those sounds together!”
Say sounds cleanly: /t/ not “tuh”, /m/ not “muh”. A stretched-out extra “uh” makes blending harder.
Help children feel the rounded mouth for /o/ and /u/ compared with the wider /a/ and /e/. A little mirror play is fun and useful.
tables:
🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to sound out short-o and short-u words slowly and clearly.
Use the tutor for a five-vowel sorting game once children have met all the vowels.
Let children who are ready ask the tutor to give them a new word to blend and read.

paragraphs: Day 5: Mixed CVC Practice & Reading Check – cat, cot, cut
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today, children will begin to:
Blend and read CVC words with all five short vowels (a, e, i, o, u).
Enjoy word-building challenges, swapping the middle vowel to make new words.
Show what they can do in a gentle one-to-one reading check.
Feel proud and ready for next week's sight words and continued alphabet play.
Materials
Letter cards for all sounds learned.
Three-box sound boxes.
Picture cards of CVC words across all five vowels.
Word-building mats and magnetic letters or letter tiles.
CVC word cards with all vowels.
Simple check-in sheet to note each child's reading (names + a few words).
Optional: audio clips; class AI tutor.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a celebratory tone - it is a proud day!
Flash all five vowels; children make each sound with its movement.
Signal: “Today we play with ALL the words we have learned this week!”
2. Review Content (10–15 min)
Show familiar pictures from the week; segment and blend each word together.
Group the words by their middle vowel sound as you go.
Compare mouth shapes: say /a/ /e/ /i/ /o/ /u/ in a row and let children feel the change.
Point to a written word and ask: “Which sound is hiding in the middle?”
3. Guided Practice (15–20 min)
Mixed blending together:
You segment a mixed word; children echo, blend, and match it to a picture.
Mix vowels freely so children listen carefully each time.
Word-Building Challenge - swap the vowel:
Keep the outside sounds, change the middle: cat → cot → cut.
Try pen → pin → pan. Read each new word proudly.
Cheer the discovery that one middle sound changes the whole word.
4. Independent Practice (15 min)
While the class does quiet reading and sorting, meet children one at a time for a gentle check.
CVC reading corner: children read simple word cards, match words to pictures, and sort by vowel.
One-to-one check: show a child two or three CVC words to read; then show a picture and ask them to segment and blend the word.
Keep the check warm and short - a friendly chat, not a test. Note what each child can do.
5. Wrap-up (5–10 min)
Gather and read a few favourite words together one last time.
Celebrate the whole week's progress - a big clap for the class!
Preview: “Next week we learn special sight words and keep playing with letters!”
Differentiation
Support
Use fewer words and extra visual and tactile cues in the check.
Let the child blend right after your model if solo blending is still hard.
Extension
Read a simple sentence: “The cat sat on the mat.”
Invite the child to build and read a brand-new CVC word of their choice.
Assessment
This is the week's main check-in - gentle and by observation.
Observe segmenting words into three sounds.
Note blending of three sounds into whole words.
Record each child's CVC reading on the check-in sheet.
Mark who is confident and who needs more blending play next week.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements when you segment and blend. Let children watch your lips - seeing the sound helps as much as hearing it.
Show the “push” with your hands: one hand per sound, then sweep them together as you blend. Children love copying this.
Keep the pace slow. Blending is hard mental work for a 5–7 year old, and doubly so in a new language.
Praise the effort and the try, not only the right answer - “Great listening!”, “You pushed those sounds together!”
Say sounds cleanly: /t/ not “tuh”, /m/ not “muh”. A stretched-out extra “uh” makes blending harder.
Children are still building English while learning to read - progress will be uneven, and that is normal.
Note names quietly; never let a child feel they are being tested. Recognition and enjoyment come first.
tables:
🤖  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor to run a mixed-word blending game while you do one-to-one checks with other children.
Let the tutor sound out any word a child finds tricky, slowly and patiently, as many times as needed.
Use the tutor to give confident children fresh CVC words to read so everyone stays happily busy.

paragraphs: Week 4: Blending Sounds (CVC Words)
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 4, children will be able to:
Segment a simple CVC word into its three separate sounds (for example, hear /s/ /u/ /n/ in “sun”).
Blend three sounds smoothly back together to say a whole word.
Read simple CVC words with all five short vowels: a, e, i, o, u.
Match a CVC word to the picture it names.
Begin to feel the joy of “word building” - changing one sound to make a new word (cat → hat → hut).
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 – Introduction to Sound Blending: review letter sounds; meet the idea of pushing sounds together; segment words and blend the first two sounds (consonant + vowel).
Day 2 – Short a CVC Words: blend and read full words such as cat, hat, map, bat, fan.
Day 3 – Short e and i CVC Words: blend and read bed, pen, leg and pin, big, sit.
Day 4 – Short o and u CVC Words: blend and read dog, top, pot and sun, cup, bug.
Day 5 – Mixed CVC Practice & Check-in: blend words with all five vowels, word-building challenges, and a gentle one-to-one reading check.
Materials for the Week
Low-cost and printable:
Letter cards for every sound learned so far (paper or card).
Three-box “sound boxes” template (draw three squares in a row) for segmenting.
Picture cards of familiar CVC objects (cat, sun, cup, dog, bed …).
Word-building mats, letter tiles, or magnetic letters (bottle-cap letters work well).
CVC word cards and simple word-picture matching worksheets.
Digital and AI:
Short audio clips of each word segmented (/c/ /a/ /t/) then blended (“cat”).
The class AI tutor to sound out CVC words aloud at a slow, steady pace and play blending games.
A tablet or screen to show a picture and its written word side by side.
Assessment This Week
Assessment is gentle and by observation - there is no test. Watch and note, across the week:
Can the child hear and say the three separate sounds in a word?
Can the child blend three sounds into a whole word?
Can the child match a read word to its picture?

On Day 5, listen to each child read two or three CVC words one-to-one. Record who is confident and who needs more play with blending next week. Recognition and enjoyment matter more than perfect reading.
tables:
💡  Week at a glance
The big idea: children hear three separate sounds and squeeze them together into one word - /k/ /a/ /t/ becomes “cat”.
This is the bridge between knowing letter sounds (Weeks 2–3) and actually reading. It is the most important week so far.
Go slowly and warmly. Reading is brand new AND it is happening in a new language - celebrate every attempt, not just correct answers.
Every day follows the same rhythm: hear the 3 sounds → blend them together → read the whole word.

paragraphs: Day 1: Sight words — I, the, a
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Warm up their alphabet knowledge and blend a familiar CVC word.
Meet the idea of a sight word — a word we just know, without sounding it out.
Recognise and say the first three sight words: I, a, the.
Spot these words both on their own and inside a short phrase.
Materials
Low-cost / printable:
Big flashcards for I, a, the (each in its own colour).
Alphabet flashcards for a quick review.
Picture cards to pair with words (cat, dog, sun, bag).
Coloured markers or highlighters; simple sentence strips with the sight words highlighted.
Digital:
A short alphabet song video or audio clip.
Optional: a simple flashcard slideshow on a tablet or class screen.
AI tutor to model clear pronunciation of each new word.
Daily Structure
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly with a wave and a smile: “Assalamu alaikum! Good morning!”
Flash 6–8 alphabet cards; children call out the letter name, then its sound.
Blend one easy CVC word together: c-a-t → cat. Cheer.
Use a happy gesture (hands up) to signal: “Today we learn something NEW!”
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Hold up the. Say: “This is a special word. It is a sight word. We do NOT sound it out — we just know it, like a friend's face.”
Model: “This word is *the*.” Children repeat it 3–4 times, together and happily.
Show I. Point to yourself: “This word is *I*. *I* means me.” Children point to themselves and say “I!”
Show a. “This word is *a*. We use it before things: *a* cat, *a* bag.” Show a picture each time.
Give each word its own colour and keep it that colour all week.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Flash & say: hold cards up in random order; children say each word together, a little faster each round.
Look–Say–Cover–Check: show the, all say it, cover it, children say it from memory, uncover to check. Repeat for I and a.
Words in context: show “I see…”, “a dog”, “the cat” with matching pictures; read each together.
Move it: give each word an action — I = point to self, a = hold up one finger, the = point right at an object. Play a quick call-and-move game.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Sight-word hunt: on a sentence strip, children find and highlight I, a, the in their word-colours.
Matching: match identical word cards (the–the, I–I) and lay them in pairs.
Finish the phrase: children place the correct word card into “___ cat” and “___ dog”.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Quick flash round of the three new words — a friendly “beat the clock” cheer.
Ask: “Which word means *me*?” (I).
Preview: “Tomorrow we meet three more special words!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on just one or two words (start with I).
Add extra picture and gesture cues; let the child trace the word with a finger.
Extension:
Let confident children copy a sight word or build “I see a ___” with cards.
Assessment
Watch who recognises each word on the flash round.
Note who joins in with the actions and matching.
Check the matching activity for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use one steady colour per sight word all week — the colour becomes a memory hook.
Show each word in isolation AND inside a phrase, so children meet it the way they will really read it.
Keep the pace slow and the mood light. Sight words are learned through many happy repetitions.
Celebrate small wins out loud — a thumbs-up, a cheer, a sticker. Confidence is the goal.
Point under each word as you read, left to right, to build one-to-one word tracking.
This is a NEW kind of learning (not blending). Say clearly that sight words are “just-know” words.
tables:
🧩  Sight words + CVC = tiny sentences
Show how a sight word joins a CVC word the children already know:
  “I  see  a  cat.”      → sight words I, a  +  CVC word cat.
  “The  dog  is  big.”  (preview; “is” comes tomorrow).
Point under each word as you read. Say: “Capital letter to start, full stop to end.”

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Please say the word *the* slowly, then in a short sentence.”
Use a flashcard app or slideshow to add gentle repetition without extra prep.
Let a child hear “I”, “a”, “the” read aloud and echo back — great for shy speakers.

paragraphs: Day 2: Sight words — and, is, to
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Review the first sight words I, a, the.
Learn three new sight words: and, is, to.
Keep recognising letters and their sounds.
Use the new words inside short spoken phrases.
Materials
Low-cost / printable:
Flashcards for the previous words (I, a, the) and the new words (and, is, to).
Alphabet flashcards; picture cards (cat, dog, sun, box).
Coloured markers/highlighters; sentence strips with sight words highlighted.
Sorting mats or two labelled trays for a sorting game.
Digital:
Alphabet song audio with actions.
Optional sight-word game on a tablet or class screen.
AI tutor for pronunciation modelling and a quick listening game.
Daily Structure
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a smile.
Flash I, a, the — children read them and do the matching actions.
Sing the alphabet song with actions.
Signal happily: “Three NEW special words today!”
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Re-show I, a, the; children read them together with their actions.
Show and. “This word is *and*.” Demonstrate: hold up two pictures — “a cat *and* a dog.”
Show is. “This word is *is*. We use it to tell what something is: *It is a cat.*”
Show to. “This word is *to*. It can show where we go: *Go to the door.*” Walk *to* something.
Give each new word its own colour and have children repeat each one several times.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Flash & say: hold up all six words in random order; children read them, a little faster each round.
Look–Say–Cover–Check with and, is, to, one at a time.
Quick alphabet review: revisit any letters/sounds the class finds tricky.
Words in context: read “a cat *and* a dog”, “The cat *is* big”, “Go *to* the mat” with pictures.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Word sort: sort cards into two trays — “words I knew” vs “new words”.
Picture match: match sight words / phrases to the right picture.
Alphabet task: match uppercase to lowercase letters; name a beginning sound for a picture.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Quick flash round of all six words.
Ask: “Which word joins two things together?” (and).
Preview: “Tomorrow we put words together to make sentences!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on fewer words (maybe just and and is).
Keep extra picture and action cues nearby.
Extension:
Let children make a short phrase with two or more sight-word cards, e.g. “a cat and a dog”.
Assessment
Watch who reads all six words on the flash round.
Note who still holds the alphabet knowledge.
Check the sorting and matching for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use one steady colour per sight word all week — the colour becomes a memory hook.
Show each word in isolation AND inside a phrase, so children meet it the way they will really read it.
Keep the pace slow and the mood light. Sight words are learned through many happy repetitions.
Celebrate small wins out loud — a thumbs-up, a cheer, a sticker. Confidence is the goal.
Point under each word as you read, left to right, to build one-to-one word tracking.
Balance sight-word work with a little alphabet review so earlier skills stay fresh.
tables:
🧩  Sight words + CVC = tiny sentences
Now we can build a little more with today's words:
  “The  cat  is  big.”        → the, is  +  CVC word cat.
  “A  cat  and  a  dog.”  → a, and.
  “Go  to  the  mat.”       → to, the  +  CVC word mat.

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Can you play a quick listening game with the words *and*, *is*, *to*?”
Use a digital sorting or matching game to add variety to independent time.
Have the tutor read a phrase like “The cat is big” so children hear natural rhythm.

paragraphs: Day 3: Sight words — in, it, you
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Review the six sight words learned so far.
Learn three new sight words: in, it, you.
Begin to understand what a sentence is.
Read a few very simple sentences together.
Materials
Low-cost / printable:
Flashcards for all words learned so far, plus in, it, you.
Sentence strips (highlighted) and small word cards for building sentences.
Picture cards to match with sentences; alphabet sound cards.
Coloured markers/highlighters.
Digital:
Optional sentence-building activity on a tablet or screen.
AI tutor to read finished sentences aloud so children hear the rhythm.
A short letter-sounds clip for review.
Daily Structure
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and smile.
Flash the six known words; children read them with actions.
Play a quick “point to the word I say” game.
Signal: “Today our words join hands to make SENTENCES!”
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Re-show all six known words; children read them together.
Show in. “This word is *in*.” Put a pencil *in* a box: “The pencil is *in* the box.”
Show it. “This word is *it*. We use it to talk about something: *It is red.*”
Show you. “This word is *you*.” Point to the children: “*You* are here!”
Show a sentence strip: “I see you.” Say: “This is a sentence — many words together. It starts with a capital and ends with a full stop.” Point under each word.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Look–Say–Cover–Check with in, it, you.
Build a sentence together: with word cards make “I see the cat.”, “It is a dog.”, “You are in the room.” Read each aloud, pointing under every word.
Alphabet sounds: quickly review the sounds that appear in the new words (i, t, y).
Sentence–picture match: match a simple sentence to the right picture and read it as a class.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Finish the sentence: drop the correct word card into a gap, e.g. “The cat is ___ the box.” (in).
Draw & match: draw a picture for a short sentence like “It is a sun.”
Word hunt: find and highlight in, it, you in sentence strips, each in its own colour.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Review all nine words in a quick flash round.
Read two simple sentences together, pointing under each word.
Preview tomorrow's new words.
Differentiation
Support:
Use shorter sentences (three words) and extra picture cues.
Let the child match rather than build, then read along.
Extension:
Invite children to build their own short sentence from the word cards and read it out.
Assessment
Watch recognition of all nine words.
Note early understanding of “what a sentence is”.
Check the sentence–picture matching for comprehension.
Teacher Notes
Use one steady colour per sight word all week — the colour becomes a memory hook.
Show each word in isolation AND inside a phrase, so children meet it the way they will really read it.
Keep the pace slow and the mood light. Sight words are learned through many happy repetitions.
Celebrate small wins out loud — a thumbs-up, a cheer, a sticker. Confidence is the goal.
Point under each word as you read, left to right, to build one-to-one word tracking.
Read sentences with natural rhythm and expression — model what fluent reading sounds like.
Sentence reading is complex; keep sentences short and celebrate every attempt.
tables:
🧩  Sight words + CVC = tiny sentences
The children can now read whole tiny sentences — the big moment of the week:
  “I  see  you.”
  “It  is  a  cat.”           → it, is, a  +  CVC word cat.
  “The  cat  is  in  the  box.”  → the, is, in  +  CVC words cat, box.

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Please read ‘I see the cat’ slowly, then a little faster.”
Use a digital drag-the-word activity so children build sentences hands-on.
Let the tutor gently praise a child's finished sentence — lovely for motivation.

paragraphs: Day 4: Sight words — for, my, we
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Review the nine sight words learned so far.
Learn three new sight words: for, my, we.
Revisit a few tricky letter sounds (phonics).
Read simple sentences that mix sight words and CVC words.
Materials
Low-cost / printable:
Flashcards for all words so far, plus for, my, we.
Sentence strips (highlighted) and picture cards.
Phonics review cards; letter-sound practice materials.
Coloured markers/highlighters.
Digital:
A short phonics/letter-sounds clip focused on tricky sounds.
Optional sight-word game on tablet or screen.
AI tutor for pronunciation and a quick sound game.
Daily Structure
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and smile.
Flash the nine known words; children read them with actions.
Play a quick sight-word identification game.
Signal: “Three new words AND some sound practice today!”
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Re-show the nine known words; children read them together.
Show for. “This word is *for*.” Give a small gift: “This is *for* you.”
Show my. “This word is *my*. It shows something is mine: *my* book.” Hold your own book.
Show we. “This word is *we*. It means me and you together.” Gesture around the group.
Repeat each word several times; keep each word's colour steady.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Phonics review: revisit tricky sounds and blend a couple of CVC words (b-a-g → bag, s-u-n → sun).
Look–Say–Cover–Check with for, my, we.
Words in context: read “This is *for* you.”, “*My* cat is big.”, “*We* see the dog.” with pictures.
Sentence reading: read short sentences and match each to its picture.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Word sort: sort all learned words into groups (e.g. by colour, or known vs newest).
Picture match: match sight words / phrases to pictures.
Phonics practice: name beginning sounds in simple pictures; blend one or two CVC words.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Quick flash round of all twelve words.
Read a couple of simple sentences together.
Preview: “Tomorrow we finish our words and celebrate!”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus on fewer words and add extra picture cues.
Pair the child with a helper for the phonics blending.
Extension:
Let children build and read a sentence using two or more sight words, e.g. “My dog can run” (preview of Day 5).
Assessment
Watch recognition of all twelve words.
Note retention of letter sounds in the phonics review.
Check sentence reading for understanding.
Teacher Notes
Use one steady colour per sight word all week — the colour becomes a memory hook.
Show each word in isolation AND inside a phrase, so children meet it the way they will really read it.
Keep the pace slow and the mood light. Sight words are learned through many happy repetitions.
Celebrate small wins out loud — a thumbs-up, a cheer, a sticker. Confidence is the goal.
Point under each word as you read, left to right, to build one-to-one word tracking.
Balance today's sight-word work with the phonics review so both skills grow together.
tables:
🧩  Sight words + CVC = tiny sentences
Mix the newest sight words with CVC words the class knows:
  “My  cat  is  big.”        → my, is  +  CVC word cat.
  “We  see  the  sun.”      → we, the  +  CVC word sun.
  “This  bag  is  for  you.”  → is, for  +  CVC word bag.

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Can you play a quick sound game with the letters b, s and m?”
Use a phonics app for a couple of minutes of playful sound review.
Have the tutor read “My cat is big” and let children echo it back.

paragraphs: Day 5: Sight words — see, can, go (review & celebrate)
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Review all twelve sight words from the week.
Learn the final three sight words: see, can, go.
Do a gentle, comprehensive alphabet and phonics review.
Take part in a friendly, low-pressure check of sight-word recognition and celebrate progress.
Materials
Low-cost / printable:
Flashcards for all 15 words.
Sentence strips (highlighted); picture cards to match.
Alphabet and phonics review materials.
Simple sight-word check sheet; a few blank certificates.
Digital:
Sight-word bingo or memory game on a tablet or screen.
AI tutor to run a fun review game and to read celebration sentences.
Optional alphabet song for the wrap-up.
Daily Structure
1.  Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Warm greeting and a big smile — it's celebration day!
Flash all twelve known words; children read them with actions.
Play a quick, joyful sight-word game.
Signal: “Three LAST special words, then we celebrate!”
2.  New Content  (10–15 min)
Re-show the twelve known words; children read them together.
Show see. “This word is *see*.” Point to your eyes, then an object: “I *see* the book.”
Show can. “This word is *can*. It shows we are able: *I can jump!*” Jump together.
Show go. “This word is *go*. It means to move.” Take a few steps: “We *go* to the door.”
Repeat each new word several times with its action.
3.  Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Comprehensive alphabet review: quick pass through letter names and sounds; linger on any tricky ones.
Sight-word sentences: read “I *can* *see* the cat.”, “We *go* to the school.”, “My dog is *in* the house.”
Games: play sight-word bingo, then a memory-match game with word cards.
4.  Independent Practice  (10–15 min)
Friendly recognition check: one at a time, show each of the 15 flashcards; the child reads the word. Note gently which come quickly and which need more time.
Sentence reading check: child reads a short sentence and matches it to the right picture.
Alphabet & phonics check: a quick look at letter recognition and a sound or two.
5.  Wrap-up  (5 min)
Cheer through all fifteen words one last time as a class.
Celebrate the week loudly — clap, thumbs-up, “You are readers now, mashallah!”
Hand out certificates for completing Week 5.
Preview: “Next week we review everything together.”
Differentiation
Support:
Focus the check on the most useful words (I, a, the, is, and).
Keep picture cues available and let children read along if a solo read feels hard.
Extension:
Invite confident children to read a short string of sentences aloud, or build their own.
Assessment
Record which of the 15 words each child recognises.
Note who can read a sight word inside a simple sentence.
Jot down which words to review again next week — this guides your planning.
Teacher Notes
Use one steady colour per sight word all week — the colour becomes a memory hook.
Show each word in isolation AND inside a phrase, so children meet it the way they will really read it.
Keep the pace slow and the mood light. Sight words are learned through many happy repetitions.
Celebrate small wins out loud — a thumbs-up, a cheer, a sticker. Confidence is the goal.
Point under each word as you read, left to right, to build one-to-one word tracking.
Keep the check informal and warm — it is a game, never a test.
Celebrate ALL progress, however small. Sight-word mastery grows gradually over time.
tables:
🧩  Sight words + CVC = tiny sentences
The full week comes together in sentences mixing many sight words with CVC words:
  “I  can  see  the  cat.”            → I, can, see, the  +  CVC word cat.
  “We  go  to  the  sun.”              → we, go, to, the  +  CVC word sun.
  “My  dog  is  in  the  box.”        → my, is, in, the  +  CVC words dog, box.

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Can you play sight-word bingo with our 15 words?”
Let the tutor read a child's sentence back and cheer — a lovely celebration moment.
Use a digital certificate template if paper ones aren't handy.

paragraphs: Week 5: First Sight Words + Alphabet Practice
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
What Sight Words Are (a quick note for the teacher)

Sight words are high-frequency words that appear again and again in English. Many cannot be easily sounded out (“the,” “you”), so children learn to recognise them as whole shapes, the way they recognise a friend's face. We teach them by seeing, saying, moving and playing — not by blending sounds. This sits happily beside the phonics work the children already know.
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 5, children will be able to:
Recognise and read 12–15 high-frequency sight words on sight.
Blend a short CVC word with a sight word to read a tiny sentence (e.g. I see a cat.).
Keep recognising letter names and their sounds from earlier weeks.
Begin to feel what a sentence is — several words that go together and start with a capital.
Feel proud and confident as early readers.
The Five Daily Focuses
Day 1 — Meet sight words: review alphabet & CVC, then learn I, a, the.
Day 2 — More words + alphabet: review, learn and, is, to, play word games.
Day 3 — Words in sentences: learn in, it, you, and build first simple sentences.
Day 4 — More words + phonics: learn for, my, we, revisit tricky sounds.
Day 5 — Review & celebrate: learn see, can, go, gentle check-in, certificates.
Materials for the Week
Sight-word flashcards for all 15 words (large, clear print). Free printables work well.
Alphabet flashcards and a familiar alphabet song or chant.
Sentence strips and small word cards for building sentences by hand.
Picture cards (cat, dog, sun, box, bag…) that pair with CVC words the children know.
Coloured markers or highlighters — use one steady colour per sight word all week.
Simple sight-word games: bingo, memory match, “word hunt” strips.
A few blank certificates for Friday.
Assessment This Week

Assessment is gentle and mostly by watching. Notice which words each child reads quickly, which need more time, and who is starting to read tiny sentences. A short, friendly one-to-one flashcard check on Day 5 tells you which words to keep reviewing next week. Keep it low-pressure — a game, not a test.
tables:
🗓️  Week at a glance
This week the children meet their very first sight words — the little words we read in a blink, without sounding them out.
They learn 15 sight words across the week (3 new each day) while we keep reviewing letters and CVC blending from earlier weeks.
The magic moment: watching sight words + CVC words click together into a first real sentence, like “I see a cat.”
Keep it warm and playful. We are aiming for recognition and joy, not perfect mastery — that grows over time.

paragraphs: Day 1: Review Letters A–Z
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Recognise all 26 letters of the alphabet, uppercase and lowercase.
Grow more confident naming letters through familiar games.
Practise forming a few letters with good pencil grip.
Show their letter recognition to the teacher in a gentle, playful check.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Full alphabet flashcards — uppercase and lowercase (printable).
Large alphabet wall chart or poster.
Letter formation practice sheets (dotted letters to trace).
Home-made alphabet puzzles and matching cards (card offcuts, bottle caps with letters).
A simple recognition checklist on one page (child names, letters A–Z).
Digital
Alphabet song audio or video on a phone, tablet, or classroom screen.
AI tutor to generate extra letter-hunt lists or a quick alphabet quiz.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet the children warmly — *Assalaamu calaykum, good morning!* — with a smile and a wave.
Sing the whole alphabet song together with actions; do it twice, faster the second time for giggles.
Flash 6–8 letter cards quickly and let the class call out the names together.
Signal with a gesture that today is a special review day — we get to show what we know!
2. Review Content  (10–15 min)
Whole-alphabet review
Point to each letter on the chart; children say the name, uppercase then lowercase.
Then point in random order to keep them listening and looking.
Give a little extra time to any letters the class found tricky in earlier weeks (often b/d, p/q, m/n).
Letter formation
Model forming two or three letters; children trace them in the air, then on a partner's back for fun.
Remind gently about pencil grip and sitting tall before writing.
3. Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Rotate through 2–3 games so the review feels like play, not testing:
Alphabet Bingo — children cover letters as you call them.
Letter Treasure Hunt — hide letter cards around the room; children find a letter and name it.
Uppercase–Lowercase Sort — match capital letters to their small partners (like a mother and her child).
Letter–Picture Match — link each letter to a beginning-sound picture (a for *apple*, m for *moon*).
4. Independent Practice & Assessment  (10–15 min)
While most children work on letter puzzles or tracing sheets, quietly call one child at a time.
Show letters one by one; the child names each. Tick your checklist — secure / developing / needs more time.
Keep it light and encouraging; a smile and *“Good trying!”* keeps confidence high.
Note which letters need a little more love so you can revisit them during the week.
5. Wrap-up  (5 min)
Quickly revisit any letter the class found hard, all together.
Celebrate: *“Look how many letters you know now!”*
Preview tomorrow — the sounds the letters make.
Differentiation
Support: Focus on the child's most secure letters first to build confidence, then a few tricky ones. Add tactile cues — sand trays, textured letters, or forming letters with dough.
Extension: Ask the child to put letters in alphabetical order, or to name a word that begins with each letter.
Assessment
Record each child's letter recognition (uppercase and lowercase) on the checklist.
Note any letters still developing.
Observe letter formation attempts — effort and control, not perfection.
Teacher Notes
Keep the whole day game-like and joyful — this is celebration, not a test.
Use plenty of specific praise: name what the child did well.
Perfect mastery is not expected at this stage; look for progress since Week 1.
Many of our children are learning English alongside Somali and Arabic — a little extra time with sounds and shapes is completely normal.
tables:
💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Play the alphabet song video for a lively, multi-sensory warm-up the children can sing along to.
Ask the AI tutor: “Give me 10 classroom objects for a letter hunt, one for each of these letters.”
Use a tablet quiz app or the AI tutor to make a quick, playful “name the letter” round for early finishers.

paragraphs: Day 2: Review Letter Sounds
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Say the sound for each of the 26 letters.
Tell apart sounds that are easy to confuse (p/b, t/d, m/n).
Hear and name the beginning sound in familiar words.
Show their letter-sound knowledge in a gentle check.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter-sound flashcards for all 26 letters.
Sound picture cards (pictures whose names begin with each sound).
Simple mouth-position pictures (lips together for /m/, teeth for /f/).
Sound-sorting mats or trays.
One-page phonics checklist.
Digital
Audio clips of each letter sound (pure sounds — /s/ not “suh”).
AI tutor to suggest picture sets for a sound-sorting game.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet warmly and settle the class with the alphabet song.
Flash letter cards; instead of the name, children make the sound.
Play a quick sound game: *“What begins with mmm?”* — children shout ideas (*moon, mum, milk*).
Gesture that today is sound review day.
2. Review Content  (10–15 min)
Whole-sound review
Show each letter; children say its sound. Model clean, short sounds and let them see your mouth.
Spend a little longer on sounds the class found tricky before.
Sound discrimination
Practise pairs that sound alike: p/b, t/d, f/v. Feel the difference — hand on throat for the “buzzy” ones.
Listen for a sound at the start, and (for stronger children) at the end of a word.
3. Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound Bingo — children cover the picture that starts with the sound you say.
I Spy with sounds — *“I spy something beginning with sss.”*
Sound Sort — group picture cards by their beginning sound.
Odd One Out — three pictures, two share a beginning sound; which one is different?
4. Independent Practice & Assessment  (10–15 min)
Children match sound pictures to letters at their tables.
Call children one at a time: show a letter → child gives the sound; make a sound → child points to the letter.
Record each child on the phonics checklist.
5. Wrap-up  (5 min)
Revisit one or two tricky sounds together.
Celebrate their listening ears!
Preview tomorrow — pushing sounds together to read words.
Differentiation
Support: Focus on a handful of clear, useful sounds. Use mirrors so children watch their own mouths, and add actions for each sound.
Extension: Ask children to hear the ending sound in a word, or to give two words that begin with a sound.
Assessment
Record ability to produce and recognise letter sounds.
Note sounds that need more practice.
Observe how well children tell similar sounds apart.
Teacher Notes
Use big, clear mouth movements and make sure every child can see your face.
Keep sounds short and pure: /t/ not “tuh” — this makes blending much easier tomorrow.
Keep it game-like, with lots of encouragement.
Some English sounds (like /p/ vs /b/) are close in Somali too — patient, repeated practice is all it takes.
tables:
💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Play pure-sound audio clips so children hear a clean model — helpful when a sound is hard to say.
Ask the AI tutor: “Give me sets of 3 picture words for a beginning-sound sorting game for /s/, /m/, and /t/.”
Record a child saying sounds on a tablet and play it back — children love hearing themselves.

paragraphs: Day 3: Review Blending CVC Words
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Push sounds together to read CVC words (like *cat, pen, sun*).
Read words using all five short vowels (a, e, i, o, u).
Build and change simple words with letter tiles.
Show their blending in a gentle check.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Letter cards and magnetic or paper letter tiles.
CVC word cards covering all five short vowels.
Picture cards matching the CVC words.
Sound boxes (three-box strips) for segmenting.
Word-building mats.
One-page blending checklist.
Digital
A blending video that models pushing sounds together.
AI tutor to generate CVC word lists sorted by vowel sound.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet warmly; quick sound review with flashcards.
Segment a few easy words together: *“s – u – n… sun!”* using the blending hand-slide.
Gesture that today is blending review day — turning sounds into words.
2. Review Content  (10–15 min)
Blending technique
Remind children how we push sounds together, using the hand motion (finger slides under each sound, then a sweep).
Model segmenting then blending 3–4 CVC words slowly, then a little faster.
Word building
Build a word with tiles (*cat*), then change one letter to make a new word (*cat → cap → cup*).
Children read each new word aloud.
3. Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sound Blending Relay — teams take turns blending a word you segment.
Word-Building Challenge — you say the sounds; children build the word with tiles.
CVC Word Bingo — cover the picture that matches the word you read.
Vowel Sort — sort CVC words by their middle vowel sound.
4. Independent Practice & Assessment  (10–15 min)
Children build CVC words from tiles and match them to pictures.
Call children individually: show a CVC word → child blends and reads it; show a picture → child segments and blends the word.
Record each child on the blending checklist.
5. Wrap-up  (5 min)
Blend one or two words together as a class.
Celebrate — *“You are reading real words!”*
Preview tomorrow — sight words.
Differentiation
Support: Stay with simple, clear patterns (words with m, s, t, a). Use sound boxes and touch each box while saying the sound. Blend the first two sounds for the child, then let them add the last.
Extension: Try CCVC words with beginning blends (*stop, flag, drum*).
Assessment
Record ability to blend sounds into words.
Note any vowel sounds or patterns that need more practice.
Observe word-building skill.
Teacher Notes
Keep sounds short and pure so they blend cleanly.
The hand-slide motion is a powerful cue — use it every time until children do it themselves.
Celebrate the very first time a child blends a whole word alone — it is a big milestone.
Perfect mastery is not expected; blending clicks at different times for different children.
tables:
💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Play a short blending video so children see and hear the sounds slide together.
Ask the AI tutor: “Give me 5 simple CVC words for each short vowel, with a picture idea for each.”
Use a letter-tile app on the tablet for children who enjoy building words on screen.

paragraphs: Day 4: Review Sight Words
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Recognise all 15 sight words quickly, on their own.
Read sight words inside simple sentences.
Match short sentences to pictures.
Show their sight-word reading in a gentle check.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
Sight-word flashcards for all 15 words: I, a, the, and, is, to, in, it, you, for, my, we, see, can, go.
Simple sentence strips using the sight words.
Picture cards to match with words and sentences.
Sight-word games — bingo, memory match.
One-page sight-word checklist.
Digital
Sight-word slideshow or flashcard app on the screen.
AI tutor to write simple, culturally-friendly sentences using the words.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet warmly; flash sight-word cards for a quick group read.
Play a quick game — hold up a word, children stand if they can read it.
Gesture that today is sight-word review day — words we know by heart.
2. Review Content  (10–15 min)
All 15 sight words
Read each word together, using the action or movement the class linked to it.
Spend a little longer on any words children still find tricky.
Remind children that some of these words we just *know* — we do not sound them out.
Sight words in sentences
Read simple sentences together: *“I can see the sun.”  “We go to school.”*
Point to each word as you read so eyes track left to right.
3. Guided Practice  (15–20 min)
Sight-Word Bingo and Memory Match with word pairs.
I Spy sight words around the classroom (on charts and labels).
Sentence Match — match a sentence strip to its picture.
Finish the Sentence — add the missing sight word to complete a sentence.
4. Independent Practice & Assessment  (10–15 min)
Children play a matching or bingo game in small groups.
Call children individually: show each of the 15 flashcards → child reads it. Then a short sentence to read.
Record each child on the sight-word checklist.
5. Wrap-up  (5 min)
Read one cheerful sentence together as a class.
Celebrate — *“You are reading sentences now!”*
Preview tomorrow — our celebration day!
Differentiation
Support: Focus on the most useful words first (I, a, the, can, see, go). Keep a colour for each word to jog visual memory, and pair words with a photo or action.
Extension: Read a short 2–3 sentence text with several sight words, or write a simple sentence of their own.
Assessment
Record ability to recognise all 15 sight words.
Note words that need more practice.
Observe simple sentence reading.
Teacher Notes
Keep a consistent colour for each sight word all week to support memory.
Sight words are learned by seeing them often — quick, frequent, cheerful practice beats long drills.
Keep it game-like with lots of praise.
Perfect mastery is not expected; some words settle faster than others.

💡  Digital tools & AI tutor

Ask the AI tutor: “Write 6 very simple sentences a 6-year-old can read, using only these sight words plus easy CVC words.”
Ask the AI tutor for a picture idea to match each sentence so the sentence-match game is ready to print.

paragraphs: Day 5: Celebration & End-of-Module Assessment
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher Lesson Plan
Daily Objectives
By the end of today's lesson, children will:
Show what they have learned across all four skills in a gentle, play-based assessment.
Celebrate their progress through Pre-Unit 0.
Receive a certificate of completion.
Feel excited and ready to begin Cambridge Global English Year 1.
Materials
Low-cost / printable
The one-page end-of-module observation checklist for each child.
Station cards: letters, sounds, CVC words, sight words.
A printed certificate of completion for every child.
Small celebration treats — stickers, star stamps.
A parent/guardian information sheet listing the skills learned.
A simple reader or story that uses learned CVC words and sight words.
Digital
A favourite alphabet or phonics song for the celebration.
AI tutor to draft the parent progress note and a short class story.
Daily Structure
1. Warm-up  (5–10 min)
Greet warmly and tell the children today is a special celebration day.
Sing a favourite song from the past six weeks.
Quick, happy round of letters, sounds, and one CVC word — everyone joins in.
2. Comprehensive Review  (15–20 min)
Quick review of letter names, sounds, one or two blended words, and a few sight words.
Play one big review game that mixes all the skills (a team quiz or a whole-class treasure hunt).
Keep the energy high and joyful.
3. End-of-Module Assessment  (15–20 min)
Set up four gentle assessment stations. Most children play and work at the stations while you rotate and quietly check each child. It should feel like more play, not a test.
Station 1 — Letters: child names uppercase and lowercase letters. *Checklist: names all 26 letters.*
Station 2 — Sounds: child gives the sound for letters you show. *Checklist: matches letters to sounds.*
Station 3 — Blending: child reads a few CVC words. *Checklist: blends simple CVC words.*
Station 4 — Sight Words: child reads a few of the 15 sight words. *Checklist: reads a few sight words.*
Mark each skill as secure / developing / needs more time. Keep your voice warm and your praise generous.
4. Celebration Activities  (15 min)
Certificate ceremony — call each child by name, hand over the certificate, and say one specific thing they did well this term.
Play a favourite class game.
Read a short story together using the CVC words and sight words the children now know — let them read the words they can!
5. Wrap-up  (5–10 min)
Look back together at how much has been learned from Week 1 to Week 6.
Tell the children a little about the exciting things coming in Year 1.
Send home the take-home practice sheet and a warm goodbye.
Differentiation
Support: Offer picture and colour cues at the stations. Focus on celebrating progress, not on what is not yet secure. Every child leaves with a certificate and a win.
Extension: Offer a slightly longer text to read, or invite the child to read a sentence aloud to the class.
Assessment
This is the end-of-module assessment. For each child, complete the observation checklist covering:
Names all 26 letters (uppercase and lowercase).
Matches letters to sounds.
Blends simple CVC words.
Reads a few sight words.
Complete each child's record and note strengths and areas for continued support.
Document readiness for Cambridge Global English Year 1.
Prepare a short, positive progress summary for parents/guardians.
Teacher Notes
Keep the assessment informal and low-pressure — protect every child's confidence.
Celebrate progress, never highlight what a child cannot yet do.
Different children will be at different levels; that is expected and fine.
Emphasise how much has been learned in just six weeks.
Give parents specific, encouraging feedback and simple ideas for practice at home — reading, singing, and talking in English for a few minutes a day.
tables:
💡  Digital tools & AI tutor
Ask the AI tutor: “Write a short, warm progress note for a parent whose child knows most letters and sounds and is starting to blend.”
Ask the AI tutor to write a 4–5 sentence celebration story using our CVC words and sight words, then print it as a class keepsake.
Play a favourite song from the module during the certificate ceremony.

🎉  Celebration & readiness for Year 1
Six weeks ago, many of these children did not know a single English letter. Today they know their letters, their sounds, they can blend words, and they can read. That is a wonderful achievement — celebrate it fully!
Reassure every child and family: it is completely normal to still be growing. Year 1 will build gently on this strong foundation.
Send children off proud, confident, and excited. Maasha Allah — well done to every child and every teacher.

paragraphs: Week 6: Alphabet & Sounds Review
Pre-Unit 0: Alphabet & Sounds  ·  Year 1  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Weekly Overview
Weekly Objectives
By the end of Week 6, children will be able to:
Recognise all 26 letters of the alphabet, uppercase and lowercase.
Match each letter to its sound (letter-sound correspondence).
Blend simple CVC words (like *cat, sun, big*) with growing confidence.
Recognise and read the 15 sight words taught in Week 5.
Show they are ready for Cambridge Global English Year 1 — settled, confident, and curious.
Daily Focus
Day 1 — Alphabet Review
Revisit all 26 letters (uppercase and lowercase) through games and songs.
Practise letter formation and celebrate recognition since Week 1.
Day 2 — Phonics Review
Revisit every letter sound and practise telling similar sounds apart (p/b, t/d).
Hear and name beginning sounds in familiar words.
Day 3 — Blending Skills Review
Push sounds together to read CVC words with all five short vowels.
Build and change words with letter tiles.
Day 4 — Sight Words Review
Read all 15 sight words quickly, and inside simple sentences.
Match short sentences to pictures.
Day 5 — Celebration & Final Assessment
Gentle assessment stations covering all four skills.
Certificate ceremony, a shared story, and a joyful farewell to Pre-Unit 0.
End-of-Module Assessment Approach
Assessment this week is warm, informal, and play-based — the children should barely notice it is happening. Across the week, observe and record each child against a simple checklist:
Names all 26 letters (uppercase and lowercase).
Matches letters to sounds.
Blends simple CVC words.
Reads a few sight words.

Use a three-point note for each: secure  /  developing  /  needs more time. Perfect mastery is not expected. The goal is a clear, kind picture of where each child is, so Year 1 teachers can support them well.
Transition to Cambridge Global English Year 1

Pre-Unit 0 has given children the foundation the Cambridge Global English Year 1 course assumes: letter names, letter sounds, early blending, and a handful of sight words. In Year 1 they will move from single sounds and words toward reading and writing short sentences and simple texts. Share each child's progress summary with parents and with the Year 1 teacher, and reassure families that continuing to read, sing, and talk in English at home — even a few minutes a day — makes a real difference.
tables:
🌟  Week at a glance
This is the final week of Pre-Unit 0 — a warm week of review, gentle assessment, and celebration.
Each day revisits one big skill through games the children already know and love, then checks that skill kindly.
By Friday every child receives a certificate and is ready to step into Cambridge Global English Year 1.
Keep it playful. Six weeks ago many children did not know a single English letter — this week we celebrate how far they have come.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 1: Welcome to School
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal's First Day”
Sagal's First Day
Today was Sagal's first day at school. She wore her new dress and held her mother's hand.
“Hello!” said a kind lady at the door. “I am your teacher. What is your name?”
“My name is Sagal,” she said softly. “I am six years old.”
“Welcome, Sagal!” said the teacher. “Come and see our classroom.”
Inside, Sagal saw many things. There were tables and chairs. There were books and crayons. There was a big clock on the wall.
“Look!” said Sagal. “A red book! And a blue pencil!”
A boy smiled at her. “Hello, my name is Liban,” he said. “Do you want to be my friend?”
“Yes!” said Sagal. She was happy now.
The teacher said, “Let us sing!” All the children sang the alphabet song: “A, B, C, D…” Sagal sang too.
Then the children did a treasure hunt. “Find something green!” said the teacher. Sagal found a green crayon. “I can see a green crayon!” she said.
At the end of the day, Sagal drew a picture of her school. She drew the tables, the chairs, and her new friend Liban.
“I like my school,” said Sagal.
On the way home, her mother asked, “What did you do today?”
Sagal smiled. “I learned new words. I made a friend. And I can sing the alphabet song!”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
What is the girl's name? How old is she?
What colours did Sagal see in the classroom?
Who did Sagal make friends with?
What did Sagal draw at the end of the day?
What do YOU like about your school?

paragraphs: Unit 1: Welcome to School
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this first unit, your child learns the words for things in the classroom, some colours, and how to say their own name and age. They meet the idea that school is a happy, welcoming place. The goal is warm, confident first steps in English — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name common classroom objects (table, chair, book, pencil…).
Name and point to colours (red, blue, green, yellow…).
Say “My name is ___. I am ___ years old. I like ___.”
Say the names of some school actions (read, write, draw, sing).
Join in the alphabet song and a school poem.
Words We Will Learn
Classroom objects
table · chair · whiteboard · ABC chart · clock · book · pencil · ruler · crayon · lunchbox
Colours
red · blue · green · yellow · orange · purple · black · white · pink · brown
People at school
teacher · friend · boy · girl
Action words
read · write · draw · sing · listen · point
Songs and Rhymes
The “Hello, School!” poem — say it together
Hello, school!  /  Tables and chairs,  /  A list of rules,  /  Books and crayons.  /  Hello, school!
Point to each thing (a table, a chair, a book) as you say its name. Say it three times — a little louder each time!
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child in English: “Hello! How are you?”
Sing the alphabet song together.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to a real object (or a picture) and say the word clearly: “table”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 words at a time. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Classroom Treasure Hunt: say a word (“Find something blue!”) and your child points to or fetches it.
Sing the “Hello, School!” poem, pointing to each object.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal's First Day”, aloud. Point to the pictures your child draws and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “What did we learn today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“My name is ______. I am ______ years old.”
“This is a ______.” (holding an object)
“It is ______.” (a colour)
“I can ______.” (read, write, draw, sing)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Points to and names 5 classroom objects.   □
Names 5 colours.   □
Says their name and age in English.   □
Joins in the alphabet song.   □
Says one thing they can do: “I can ______.”   □
tables:
🧭  About this unit (please read first)
Your child is a beginner, about 5–7 years old, and is just starting to read. So YOU lead this unit — a teacher in class or a parent at home.
Work in short, happy sessions of about 15–20 minutes. Lots of pointing, saying, singing, colouring and playing — very little writing.
This guide tells you what to do. The Child Activity Sheet is what your child draws and colours. The Story is one you read aloud.

💬  Using the AI Tutor with a young child
The grown-up holds the device and reads with the child — do not leave a 5-year-old alone with it.
Ask the tutor to say a word slowly so your child can copy the sound: “Please say the word ‘crayon’ slowly.”
Ask it to sing the alphabet song, or to play a colour-guessing game with you and your child.
Ask it to give you one more simple activity if your child wants to keep going.

🌟  Tips for teaching absolute beginners
Keep it short and playful. Stop while it is still fun.
Repeat, repeat, repeat — young children learn by hearing words many times.
Use your hands, face and voice. Point, act, and smile.
Praise every try. Confidence matters more than being correct at this stage.

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 2: Family Time
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Breakfast at Grandma's House”
Breakfast at Grandma's House
It was Saturday morning. Sagal woke up early. “We are going to Grandma's house!” she said.
Sagal has a big family. She has a mother and a father. She has a little brother named Yusuf. Yusuf is a baby. He is very small.
“Come, Sagal,” said her mum. “Hold my hand.” They walked to Grandma's house together.
Grandma opened the door. “Hello, my dear ones!” she said. She gave everyone a big hug.
“I love you, Grandma,” said Sagal.
Grandpa was in the kitchen. “Good morning!” he said. “Who is hungry? It is time to eat!”
“Me! Me!” said Sagal. She was very hungry.
On the table there was lots of fruit. There was one mango. There was one yellow banana. There were ten green grapes. There were four red strawberries.
“Let us count the grapes,” said Grandpa. Sagal counted: “one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten!” Ten grapes!
“Well done, Sagal!” said Grandpa.
“Can I help?” asked Sagal. “Yes, please,” said Grandma. “You can lay the table.” Sagal put out the little bowls. One, two, three, four, five bowls.
Everyone sat down. They ate the sweet mango. They ate the soft banana. Baby Yusuf ate his cereal with milk. Some of it went on his nose! Everyone began to laugh.
“Yusuf is a funny baby,” said Sagal, and she gave him a kiss.
After breakfast, the family sat together. Mum and Dad talked with Grandma and Grandpa. Sagal helped to tidy the bowls. Then she played a counting game with Yusuf.
“I love Saturday,” said Sagal. “I love my family best of all.”
Grandma smiled. “And your family loves you, little one,” she said.
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
Who is in Sagal's family?
Where did they go on Saturday morning?
How many grapes were on the table? Can you count them?
How did Sagal help Grandma?
Who is in YOUR family? How do you help them?

paragraphs: Unit 2: Family Time
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns the words for family members (mum, dad, sister, brother, grandma, grandpa), some breakfast and fruit words, and how to count from 1 to 10. They talk about who is in their family and how families help and have fun together. The gentle idea is that families love and help each other. The goal is warm, confident talking in English — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name the people in a family (mother, father, sister, brother, grandma, grandpa, baby).
Name some breakfast foods and fruits (pancakes, cereal, milk, mango, banana, grapes).
Count from 1 to 10, and count backwards from 10 to 1.
Ask and answer simple questions: “Do you like bananas?” → “Yes, I do.” / “No, I don't.”
Say what their family does together (help, work, play, eat, talk, laugh).
Hear the short ‘a’ sound in words like cat, hat and map.
Join in the “Families” poem and the “Ten in the Bed” counting song.
Words We Will Learn
Family members
mother / mum · father / dad · sister · brother · grandma · grandpa · family · baby · grown-ups · children
Breakfast and food
pancakes · cereal · milk · noodles · fruit · mango · banana · grapes · strawberries · watermelon
Numbers
one · two · three · four · five · six · seven · eight · nine · ten
Family actions
help · work · play · eat · talk · laugh · lay the table · tidy your room
Songs and Rhymes
The “Families” poem — say it together
Some families are big,  /  Some families are small.  /  I love my family best of all.
After you say it, ask your child: “Is our family big or small? Who is in our family?” Say the poem three times — a little louder each time!
The “Ten in the Bed” counting song

There are 10 in the bed  /  And the little one says, ‘Roll over. Roll over.’  /  So they all roll over and 1 falls out…  (count down to 1)  /  There is 1 in the bed  /  And the little one says, ‘Good night!’
Hold up ten fingers and put one down each time. This is a fun way to practise counting backwards.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! How are you today?”
Count together from 1 to 10 on your fingers.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to a family photo (or a picture) and say the word clearly: “mum”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 words at a time. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
“How Many?” game: point around the room and ask “How many chairs are there?” Count them together.
Sing “Ten in the Bed”, putting a finger down for each one that rolls out.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Breakfast at Grandma's House”, aloud. Point to the pictures your child draws and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “Who is in your family? What do we do together?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“This is my ______.” (mum, dad, sister, brother)
“I eat ______ for breakfast.”
“Do you like ______?” → “Yes, I do.” / “No, I don't.”
“How many ______ are there?” → “There are ______.”
“I help my family. I can ______.” (tidy, play, lay the table)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Names 4 family members.   □
Counts from 1 to 10.   □
Counts backwards from 10 to 1.   □
Answers “Do you like…?” with “Yes, I do.” or “No, I don't.”   □
Says one way they help their family.   □
tables:
🧭  About this unit (please read first)
Your child is a beginner, about 5–7 years old, and is just starting to read. So YOU lead this unit — a teacher in class or a parent at home.
Work in short, happy sessions of about 15–20 minutes. Lots of pointing, saying, singing, counting and colouring — very little writing.
This guide tells you what to do. The Child Activity Sheet is what your child draws and colours. The Story is one you read aloud.

💬  Using the AI Tutor with a young child
The grown-up holds the device and reads with the child — do not leave a 5-year-old alone with it.
Ask the tutor to say a family word slowly so your child can copy the sound: “Please say the word ‘grandma’ slowly.”
Ask it to count from 1 to 10 with you, or to sing the “Ten in the Bed” song.
Ask it to play a “Do you like…?” game about fruit with you and your child.
Ask it to give you one more simple activity if your child wants to keep going.

🌟  Tips for teaching absolute beginners
Keep it short and playful. Stop while it is still fun.
Repeat, repeat, repeat — young children learn by hearing words many times.
Use your hands, face and voice. Point, count on fingers, act, and smile.
Use real family photos and real fruit at home — it makes the words come alive.
Praise every try. Confidence matters more than being correct at this stage.

paragraphs: My Fun and Games Book
Year 1 · Unit 3 · Move, draw, and play!
(For the grown-up: read each instruction aloud to your child and help them enjoy it. There are no wrong answers.)
1.  Draw your favourite game or toy. Then say: “I like to play with my ______.”
2.  Colour the ball. Then do the action! Bounce, roll and catch a real ball or a pretend one.
⚽  Colour the big ball, then say: bounce · roll · throw · catch
(For the grown-up: say each action word and do it together — pretend to bounce, roll, throw and catch.)
3.  Match the body parts. Draw a line from the word to the right spot on the body.
nose   ·   ear   ·   hand   ·   foot
(For the grown-up: point to each body part on your own body first, then help your child match.)
4.  Trace your name. Then draw yourself jumping!
5.  Point and say. Point to each animal and say its name and its action.
🐰 rabbit — hop    🦆 duck — run    🐸 frog — jump    🐝 bee — buzz
(For the grown-up: act out each animal action with your child — hop, run, jump, buzz!)
6.  Where is the ball? Colour the ball ON the box, UNDER the box, and NEXT TO the box.
on          under          next to

(bounce · jump · clap · run)

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 3: Fun and Games
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal and the Big Ball”
Sagal and the Big Ball
It was a sunny day in the town of Hargeisa. Sagal ran outside to play. She had a big red ball.
“Look at my ball!” said Sagal. She could bounce it. Bounce, bounce, bounce!
Her friend Amina came to play. “Can you catch the ball?” asked Sagal.
“Yes, I can!” said Amina. Sagal did throw the ball. Amina did catch it. “Well done!” said Sagal.
Then little Yusuf came. He was only four. “Can I play too?” asked Yusuf.
Sagal looked at the ball. She wanted to play with Amina. But Yusuf looked sad.
“Yes!” said Sagal. “Let us all play. We can take turns.”
Sagal did roll the ball to Yusuf. Roll, roll, roll! Yusuf put his two hands on the ball. He was happy.
“Now throw it to me!” said Amina. Yusuf did throw the ball. But — oh no! The ball went up, up, up!
The ball went into a big tree. It was on a branch. “My ball is stuck!” said Sagal.
The three friends looked up. How could they get the ball?
“I know!” said Amina. “Let us jump!” They all did jump. Jump, jump, jump! But the ball was too high.
Just then, big brother Liban came. He was tall. “I can help,” said Liban.
Liban did shake the branch. Shake, shake, shake! The ball came down. Sagal did catch it. “Thank you, Liban!” said all the children.
“You shared the ball,” said Liban. “You are good friends.”
Sagal, Amina and Yusuf played all day. They did bounce and roll and catch. It was so much fun!
When the sun went down, Sagal said, “Sharing is the best game of all.”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
What colour was Sagal's ball? What could she do with it?
Who wanted to play too? What did Sagal say?
Where did the ball get stuck?
How did Liban help get the ball down?
Why is it good to take turns and share?

paragraphs: Unit 3: Fun and Games
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns the words for games and play — action words like bounce, roll, throw and catch. They learn the names of parts of the body, and words that say where things are (on, under, next to), and left and right. They play simple games, learn a game from another country, and listen to a funny story. Along the way they hear the short 'u' sound (sun, fun, duck). The goal is happy, active English — learning through play.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name and act out ball actions (bounce, roll, throw, catch, hit).
Name parts of the body (hand, foot, ear, nose, shoulder, head…).
Say where something is (on, under, next to) and show left and right.
Ask and answer “Can you ___?” with “Yes, I can.” / “No, I can't.”
Follow simple game instructions and take turns fairly.
Hear and say words with the short ‘u’ sound (sun, fun, duck, bug).
Join in the “Bounce the Ball” poem and the “Wind the Bobbin Up” song.
Words We Will Learn
Games and action words
bounce · roll · throw · catch · hit · run · jump · clap · shake · touch · point · wind up
Parts of the body
hand · foot / feet · ear · nose · shoulder · toes · finger · head · arm · leg
Where things are (positions)
on · under · next to · left · right
Animals in our stories
rabbit · duck · frog · lion · puppy · bee · bug
Songs and Rhymes
The “Bounce the Ball” poem — say it together
Bounce the ball,  /  Ball, ball,  /  Bounce the ball!  /  Roll it, throw it,  /  Catch the ball!
Clap the rhythm as you say it. If you have a ball, bounce it as you read. Say it three times — a little faster each time!
The “Wind the Bobbin Up” action song

Wind the bobbin up.  /  Wind the bobbin up.  /  Pull, pull. Clap, clap, clap.  /  Point to the ceiling.  /  Point to the floor.  /  Point to the window and point to the door.  /  Clap your hands together, 1, 2, 3.  /  Put your hands upon your knees.
Do every action as you sing. Point to the ceiling, the floor, the window and the door. This is a wonderful way to learn body and action words with your whole body.
Little rhyme: “I can run like a puppy” (short ‘u’ sound)
I can run like a puppy.  /  I can jump like a bug.  /  I can buzz like a bee.  /  I can sit on the rug.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! Are you ready to play?”
Sing “Wind the Bobbin Up” and do the actions together.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Say an action word and DO it: “bounce” (pretend to bounce a ball), “clap”, “jump”. Ask your child to copy you.
Touch a body part and name it: “nose”, “ear”, “foot”. Do 4–5 words at a time, with big smiles and praise.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Play “Simon Says” with body parts: “Point to your shoulder! Touch your nose!”
Play a simple “Can you ___?” game: “Can you catch a big ball? Can you jump 3 times?”
Say the “Bounce the Ball” poem, clapping the rhythm.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal and the Big Ball”, aloud. Point to the words in bold and to any pictures your child has drawn, and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “What did we play today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking and doing come before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I can ______.” (bounce, roll, throw, jump, clap)
“Can you ______?”  —  “Yes, I can.” / “No, I can't.”
“Point to your ______.” (nose, ear, foot, shoulder)
“The ball is ______ the box.” (on / under / next to)
“This is my ______ hand.” (left / right)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Names and acts out 4 action words (bounce, roll, throw, catch).   □
Points to and names 5 parts of the body.   □
Says where something is: on, under, next to.   □
Shows their left hand and right hand.   □
Answers “Can you ___?” with “Yes, I can.” or “No, I can't.”   □
Hears a short ‘u’ word (sun, fun, duck).   □

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 4: Making Things
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal Makes a Mat”
Sagal Makes a Mat
It was a warm afternoon in the village. Sagal sat with her grandmother, Ayeeyo, under the big tree.
Ayeeyo was making a mat from soft grass. Her hands moved fast. Over, under, over, under.
“I want to make a mat too!” said Sagal.
“Good,” said Ayeeyo. “Here is some grass. We will make it together.”
Sagal took some grass. She started to weave. Over, under, over, under.
But her mat was not neat. It was bumpy and full of holes.
“Oh no!” said Sagal. “My mat is not good.”
Her big brother Yusuf walked by. He was carrying paper and crayons.
“Do not be sad,” said Yusuf. “Look, I am making a picture with shapes. I cut a red circle and a blue square.”
He showed Sagal his paper. There was a green triangle and a yellow rectangle too.
“It is beautiful,” said Sagal. “But my first picture was not so good either!” said Yusuf. He laughed. “I tried again and again.”
Sagal looked at her bumpy mat. She took a deep breath.
“I will try again,” she said.
Over, under, over, under. Slow and careful this time. Sagal did not rush.
Little by little, the mat grew. The holes were gone. It was flat and neat.
“Ayeeyo, look!” said Sagal.
Ayeeyo smiled a big smile. “You did it, Sagal! You were patient. That is how we make good things.”
Sagal held up her mat. It was red and brown, like the earth.
Her little sister Idil clapped her hands. “I want to make a mat too!”
“Come,” said Sagal. “I will help you. Over, under, over, under…”
And under the big tree, the whole family sat and made things together, all afternoon long.
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
What was Sagal trying to make?
Why was Sagal sad at first?
What shapes did Yusuf cut for his picture?
What did Sagal do when her mat went wrong? (She tried again!)
Have you ever made something that was hard? What did you do?

paragraphs: Unit 4: Making Things
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns the words for shapes, colours, and clothes, and how to make things with paper shapes — like a paper quilt, a picture, or a shape animal. They talk about what people are wearing and dress up in pretend party costumes. They meet the short ‘e’ sound (hen, pen, tent) and enjoy the old story of the elves and the shoemaker. The gentle idea running through it all is that making things takes creativity and patience — and it is fun to try, try again.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name and point to shapes (square, circle, triangle, rectangle).
Name and point to colours (red, blue, green, yellow, silver, gold…).
Name some clothes and costumes (hat, shirt, dress, clown, king…).
Say “I’m cutting ______.” and “I’m making a ______.”
Say “I’m wearing ______.” to describe party clothes.
Hear and say words with the short ‘e’ sound (hen, pen, ten, wet).
Join in the “Party Time” poem and the “London Bridge” song.
Words We Will Learn
Shapes
square · circle · triangle · rectangle
Colours
red · blue · green · yellow · orange · purple · black · white · silver · gold
Clothes and costumes
hat · shirt · dress · jacket · trousers · shoes · boots · glasses · clown · princess · king · superhero · pilot · cook
Making words (actions)
cut · make · paint · draw · wear · help · thank · sell
Songs and Rhymes
The “Party Time” poem — say it together
Party time,  /  Look at me.  /  What do you see?  /  I can be a clown  /  With a funny frown.  /  Or a king  /  With a silver ring.
Act it out! Make a funny frown for the clown. Put on a pretend crown for the king. Say it three times — a little sillier each time!
The “Higgledy Piggledy” rhyme — for the short ‘e’ sound
Higgledy Piggledy,  /  my black hen.  /  She lays eggs  /  for gentlemen.  /  Sometimes nine and sometimes ten.
Listen for the ‘e’ sound in hen, eggs, gentlemen and ten. Flap your arms like a hen while you say it!
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child in English: “Hello! How are you today?”
Sing the “Party Time” poem together.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Hold up a paper shape (or point to a picture) and say the word clearly: “circle”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 words at a time — shapes one day, colours the next, clothes the next. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
3. Make and play (5 min)
Cut simple paper shapes together. As you cut, say: “I’m cutting a blue square.”
Make a little picture or paper quilt from the shapes. Say: “I’m making a picture.”
Play a Shape Hunt: “Find something round like a circle!” Your child points to or fetches it.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal Makes a Mat”, aloud. Point to the words in bold and to any pictures your child has drawn, and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them. Making comes first — writing can be just a letter or a mark.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “What did we make today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I’m cutting a ______.” (a red circle, a blue square)
“I’m making a ______.” (a picture, a quilt, a shape animal)
“This is a ______.” (a triangle, a rectangle)
“I’m wearing ______.” (a hat, a jacket)
“I can be a ______.” (clown, king, pilot)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Points to and names 4 shapes (square, circle, triangle, rectangle).   □
Names 5 or more colours.   □
Says “I’m cutting ______.” and “I’m making a ______.”   □
Names 3 clothes and says “I’m wearing ______.”   □
Hears the short ‘e’ sound in hen, pen, ten.   □
Joins in the “Party Time” poem or a song.   □
