paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 5: On the Farm
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal and the Little Hen”
Sagal and the Little Hen
Sagal lived near a small farm. Her grandmother, Ayeeyo, kept animals there. Sagal loved to visit every morning before the sun grew hot.
“Come, Sagal,” said Ayeeyo. “There is much work to do on the farm today.”
First they went to see the cow. “Good morning, cow!” said Sagal. The big brown cow said, “Moo!”
Next they saw the sheep and the goat. The sheep said, “Baa!” The goat wanted to eat Sagal's hat! Sagal laughed and laughed.
“Now we must feed the hens,” said Ayeeyo. She gave Sagal a small bowl of seed.
Sagal walked to the barn. The little hen looked hungry. “The hen is hungry,” said Sagal. “I will feed her.” She threw the seed on the ground. “Cluck, cluck!” said the happy hen.
Then Sagal saw something warm in the straw. It was a little white egg!
“Ayeeyo, look!” called Sagal. “The hen gave us an egg!”
“Well done, Sagal,” said Ayeeyo. “When you are kind to the animals, they are kind to you.”
In the afternoon, they worked in the field. Sagal helped to plant a tiny seed in the ground. “Will it grow?” she asked.
“Yes,” said Ayeeyo. “With water and sun, the seed will grow into a big bean plant. Everything on a farm grows — the chick grows from an egg, and the bean grows from a seed.”
Her big brother Yusuf drove past on the tractor. “Hard work, little sister!” he called. Sagal waved. She was tired, but she was happy.
That night, Sagal ate warm bread and drank fresh milk from the cow. “I love the farm, Ayeeyo,” she said softly.
Ayeeyo smiled. “And the farm loves you, Sagal. Good animals need good, kind children like you.”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
Whose farm did Sagal visit? What is her grandmother's name?
What sound did the cow make? What sound did the sheep make?
What did Sagal give the little hen to eat?
What surprise did the hen give Sagal?
How can WE be kind to animals?

paragraphs: Unit 5: On the Farm
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns about the farm. They name farm animals and the sounds they make, learn farm words like tractor, barn and field, and talk about what people do on a farm — feeding, planting and picking. They meet the idea that animals and plants grow (a chick from an egg, a bean from a seed), and they hear a gentle story about helping and hard work. The goal is warm, confident spoken English — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name common farm animals (cow, hen, chick, duck, sheep, goat, horse…).
Say the sound each animal makes (moo, cluck, quack, baa…).
Name farm places and things (farm, barn, field, tractor, egg, seed).
Say what people and animals are doing: “The hen is eating.”
Tell the simple life cycle of a hen (egg → chick → hen).
Join in the “Old MacDonald” song and the farm poem.
Words We Will Learn
Farm animals
cow · hen · chick · duck · goose · pig · sheep · horse · goat
Animal sounds
moo · cluck · quack · baa · neigh · oink · honk
Farm places and things
farm · barn · field · stall · tractor · wheat · seed · egg
Farm activities
feeding · planting · picking · driving · carrying · growing
Vegetables that grow
tomatoes · onions · potatoes · carrots · beans
Songs and Rhymes
“Old MacDonald Had a Farm” — sing it together

Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O.  /  And on that farm he had a cow, E-I-E-I-O.  /  With a moo moo here and a moo moo there,  /  Here a moo, there a moo, everywhere a moo moo!  /  Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O.
Sing a new verse for each animal your child names. Make the animal sound loudly and happily each time — a hen (cluck), a duck (quack), a sheep (baa).
The “Farm Poem” — say it together
Hello to the farmer,  /  Hello to the tractor,  /  Hello to the cows in their stall.  /  Hello to the fields,  /  Hello to the chicks,  /  Hello to you all!
Point to each thing (the tractor, the cows, the fields, the chicks) as you say its name.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! Let's go to the farm today!”
Sing “Old MacDonald” with big animal sounds.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to a farm animal (a picture or a toy) and say the word clearly: “cow”. Ask your child to repeat, then make the sound: “moo”.
Do 4–5 animals at a time. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Animal Sounds Game: you make a sound (“Baa!”) and your child names the animal (“sheep!”).
Act It Out: pretend to feed the hens or drive the tractor; your child guesses what you are doing.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal and the Little Hen”, aloud. Point to the bold words and to any pictures your child has drawn, and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “What lives on the farm?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“This is a ______.” (a farm animal)
“The ______ says ______.” (the cow says moo)
“The ______ is ______ing.” (the hen is eating)
“I can see a ______ on the farm.”
“I am ______ing.” (feeding, planting, picking)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Points to and names 5 farm animals.   □
Makes the sound for 3 animals (moo, cluck, quack).   □
Names 3 farm things (barn, tractor, egg).   □
Says what an animal is doing: “The hen is eating.”   □
Joins in the “Old MacDonald” song.   □

paragraphs: My Five Senses Book
Year 1 · Unit 6 · Look, listen, smell, taste, touch — and say!
(For the grown-up: read each instruction aloud to your child and help them enjoy it. There are no wrong answers.)
1.  Match the sense to the body part. Draw a line from each body part to what it does.
👁 eyes  →  see        👂 ears  →  hear        👃 nose  →  smell
👅 tongue  →  taste        ✋ hands  →  touch
(For the grown-up: touch each body part on your child, then say the sense together.)
2.  Draw something you love to LOOK at. Then say: “I can see a ______.”
3.  Colour the things you can smell. Which one is your favourite smell?
🍞 bread        🌺 flower        🍊 orange        🍛 food
(For the grown-up: talk about smells your child likes; help them finish “My favourite smell is ______.”)

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 6: My Five Senses
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal at the Market”
Sagal at the Market
One sunny morning, Sagal went to the market with her grandmother, Hodan.
The market was big and busy. “Come, Sagal,” said Hodan. “Use your five senses today!”
First, Sagal used her eyes to see. She saw red tomatoes and yellow bananas. She saw green mangoes in a big basket. “I can see so many colours!” said Sagal.
Next, Sagal used her ears to hear. She heard a man calling, “Fresh fish! Fresh fish!” She heard goats and a baby crying. “It is very loud here!” she said.
Then Sagal used her nose to smell. She smelled warm bread. She smelled sweet flowers. She smelled hot spices from a big pot. “Mmm! This smells so good!” said Sagal.
A kind man named Yusuf gave Sagal a piece of ripe mango. She used her tongue to taste it. “It is sweet and juicy!” she said. “Thank you, Yusuf!”
Last, Sagal used her hands to touch. She touched a soft cloth. It felt soft. She touched a smooth stone. It felt hard and cold. “I like to touch things,” said Sagal.
“Look, Grandmother!” said Sagal. “This melon is big. But that melon is bigger!”
Hodan smiled. “Well done, Sagal. You are using all your senses.”
On the way home, Sagal held her grandmother's hand. “I saw pretty colours. I heard loud sounds. I smelled sweet bread. I tasted juicy mango. And I touched soft cloth,” she said.
“Alhamdulillah,” said Hodan. “We must be thankful for our wonderful senses.”
“Yes,” said Sagal happily. “Thank you, Allah, for my eyes, my ears, my nose, my tongue, and my hands!”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
Where did Sagal go with her grandmother?
What did Sagal see at the market? What colours?
What did the mango taste like? (sweet and juicy)
Which things felt soft, hard, or cold?
What are YOU thankful for that you can see, hear, smell, taste or touch?

paragraphs: Unit 6: My Five Senses
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns about their five senses — seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting and touching — and the body parts we use for each one. They learn describing words (soft, loud, sweet, cold) and start to compare things (a tree is tall, a building is taller). Most of all, they explore the world around them and learn to be curious and thankful for all the wonderful things they can sense.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name the five senses: see, hear, smell, taste, touch.
Point to and name the body parts we use: eyes, ears, nose, tongue, hands.
Say which sense goes with which body part (“I see with my eyes”).
Use describing words: soft, hard, loud, quiet, sweet, cold.
Compare two things (tall / taller; sweet / sweeter).
Join in the “Two Little Eyes” poem and “The Music Man” song.
Words We Will Learn
The five senses
see · hear · smell · taste · touch
Body parts we use
eyes · ears · nose · tongue · mouth · hands · fingers
Describing words
soft · hard · loud · quiet · sweet · cold · juicy · round · flat · short · long
Comparing words
taller · faster · louder · quieter · sweeter · colder · juicier
Songs and Rhymes
“Two Little Eyes” — say it together
Two little eyes to see all around.  /  Two little ears to hear each sound.  /  One little nose to smell what's sweet.  /  One little mouth that likes to eat.
Point to your eyes, ears, nose and mouth as you say each line. Then ask: which sense is NOT in the poem? (Touch! Wiggle your fingers.)
“The Music Man” — sing and pretend to play
I am the music man. I come from down your way. And I can play. What can you play?  /  I can play the piano, the piano, the piano. I can play the piano, pia-piano.
Pretend to play each instrument as you sing — piano, drum, violin. This helps your child listen for different sounds.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! How are you today?”
Sing “Two Little Eyes”, pointing to each body part.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Touch a body part and say the sense: “These are my eyes. I can see.” Ask your child to copy you.
Do the five senses one at a time. Use real things — smell an orange, feel a soft cloth, taste a sweet date.
3. Play and explore (5 min)
“Touch and Tell”: put an object in a bag. Your child feels it and says “It feels soft/hard/long.”
“Shake and Listen”: shake a box and guess what is inside by the sound.
Compare two foods: “Which is sweeter?” “Which is colder?”
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal at the Market”, aloud. Point to the words in bold and to any pictures your child draws. Ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “What did we sense today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I can see with my eyes.”  “I can hear with my ears.”
“I can smell with my nose.”  “I can taste with my tongue.”
“I can touch with my hands.”
“It is ______.” (soft, hard, loud, sweet, cold)
“A tree is tall. A building is ______.” (taller)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Names the five senses: see, hear, smell, taste, touch.   □
Points to eyes, ears, nose, tongue and hands.   □
Says which body part goes with each sense.   □
Uses a describing word: “It is soft/loud/sweet.”   □
Compares two things: “This one is taller/sweeter.”   □
Joins in the “Two Little Eyes” poem.   □

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 7: Let's Go!
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “Sagal's Big Bus Ride”
Sagal's Big Bus Ride
Today, Sagal was going to see her grandmother. Grandmother lived by the sea in a town far away.
“How will we get there?” asked Sagal.
“First we walk. Then we go by bus,” said Mum. “Let's go!”
Sagal and Mum walked down the road. They walked past the market. They walked to the bus stop.
Soon a big bus came. It was yellow and red. “Beep, beep!” went the bus.
“Find a seat. Then sit down,” said Mum. Sagal found a seat by the window.
The wheels on the bus went round and round. The bus began to drive through the town.
“Look, Mum! A boy on a bicycle!” said Sagal. The boy waved. His name was Liban.
“And look — a red car!” said Sagal. The car went fast. But the bus went slow and bumpy.
Sagal saw a girl named Amina, who was going to school. Amina waved too. Sagal waved back.
The bus drove past the shops. It drove past the mosque. It drove all the way to the sea.
“We are here!” said Mum. At the sea, Sagal saw a little blue boat. The boat could float on the water.
And there, by the boat, was Grandmother! “Sagal!” she called. “You came to see me!”
“Yes!” said Sagal. She ran and gave Grandmother a big hug.
“How did you travel here?” asked Grandmother.
“We walked, and then we came by bus!” said Sagal. “The wheels went round and round!”
Grandmother smiled. “What a good journey. Now, shall we go on the boat?”
“Yes, yes!” said Sagal. She held Grandmother's hand. “Let's go!”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
Who was Sagal going to see?
How did Sagal and Mum travel? (First they… then they…)
What colour was the bus? What did the wheels do?
What did Sagal see from the window? (a bicycle, a car, a boat…)
How do YOU like to travel with your family?

paragraphs: Unit 7: Let's Go!
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns how we travel and get around. They meet the words for ways to travel (walk, bus, car, bicycle, boat) and learn to say how they go to school. They talk about journeys — going somewhere with the people they love, staying safe, and enjoying the trip. The goal is warm, confident English about the everyday world of going places — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name common ways to travel (walk, bus, car, bicycle, boat, train, plane).
Say how they get to school: “I go to school by ___.”
Say the action words for travel (drive, ride, fly, float, walk).
Use simple describing words for vehicles (big, little, fast, slow, and colours).
Join in the transport songs and rhymes, doing the actions.
Words We Will Learn
Ways to travel
walk · bus · car · bicycle · boat · train · plane · helicopter
Vehicle parts
wheels · seat · sails · bell · wipers
Action words (how things move)
drive · ride · fly · float · walk · travel · sit down · buckle up
Place and direction words
school · town · road · up · down · fast · slow · stop · go
Describing words
big · little · long · fast · slow · red · blue · green · yellow
Songs and Rhymes
“The Wheels on the Bus” — sing it together
The wheels on the bus go round and round,  /  round and round, round and round.  /  The wheels on the bus go round and round,  /  all day long!
Roll your hands round and round for the wheels. Then do more verses with actions: the wipers go “swish, swish, swish”, the bell goes “ding, ding, ding”. Sing it a little louder each time!
“Bus Driver, Bus Driver” — a little rhyme
Bus driver, bus driver,  /  may I have a ride?  /  Yes, of course.  /  Please come inside.  /  Find a seat.  /  Then sit down.  /  Buckle up.  /  We'll drive through the town.
Take turns: your child asks “May I have a ride?” and you answer. Pretend to find a seat, sit down and buckle up together.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! Are you ready to go?”
Sing “The Wheels on the Bus” together.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to a picture or toy vehicle and say the word clearly: “bus”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 words at a time. Make the sound and the action — “drive” (hold the wheel), “fly” (arms out).
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Travel sorting game: name a vehicle and ask “Does it go on land, on water, or in the sky?”
Pretend journey: line up chairs as a “bus”, buckle up, and drive through the town.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “Sagal's Big Bus Ride”, aloud. Point to the bold words and to the pictures your child has drawn, and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “How do YOU like to travel?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I go to school by ______.” (bus, car, bicycle, foot)
“Let's go!”
“It is a ______.” (a bus, a car, a boat)
“It goes ______.” (fast, slow, round and round)
“I can ______.” (walk, ride, drive, fly, float)
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Points to and names 5 ways to travel.   □
Says how they get to school: “I go to school by ______.”   □
Says a colour and size for a vehicle: “a big red bus.”   □
Joins in “The Wheels on the Bus” with actions.   □
Says one action: “I can ______.” (ride, drive, fly, float)   □

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 8: Wonderful Water
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “The Well in the Village”
The Well in the Village
Sagal lived in a small village. Near her house there was a deep well. The water in the well was cool and clean.
Every morning, Sagal walked to the well with her big brother Yusuf. They carried an empty pot.
“Fill it slowly,” said Yusuf. “Water is precious. We must not waste one drop.”
Sagal helped pull the rope. Up came the pot, full and heavy. “We did it!” she said.
At home, Mama used the water. “We use water to cook,” she said, making warm soup.
“And we use water to wash,” said Sagal. She washed her hands until they were clean.
“And we use water to drink,” said little Amina. She drank a big cup and smiled.
One day, the land was very dry. No rain came. The grass was brown. The plants did not grow.
“The well is getting low,” said Yusuf. “We must share and be careful.”
So the whole village shared the water. Nobody wasted a single drop.
Then, one afternoon, the sky went grey. Big clouds came. Drip… drip… drip…
“Rain!” shouted Sagal. “It is raining!”
The children ran outside and danced in the rain. The rain filled the river. The rain filled the well.
The brown grass turned green again. The plants began to grow. The animals came to drink.
That night, Sagal looked at the full well. “Thank you, Allah, for the water,” she said softly. “Water is wonderful.”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
Where did Sagal get her water? (a well)
What did Yusuf say we must not waste? (a drop of water)
Name three ways the family used water. (drink, wash, cook)
What happened when the rain finally came?
How do YOU use water at home? Should we waste it?

paragraphs: Unit 8: Wonderful Water
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns about water — where it comes from, where we find it, and all the ways we use it every day. They meet rainy and sunny weather, animals that live in water, and the big idea that water is a gift we should not waste. The goal is warm, confident English and a thankful heart for water — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Say why plants, animals, and people all need water.
Name places where we find water (rain, river, sea, well).
Say how we use water: “I use water to drink / wash / cook.”
Name some animals that live in water (fish, frog, turtle, whale).
Talk about the weather: “It is rainy.” “It is sunny.”
Join in a water poem and the “Row Your Boat” song.
Words We Will Learn
Water words
water · rain · drop · wet · dry · clean · float · sink
Where we find water
rain · river · sea · well
Ways we use water
drink · wash · cook · grow plants
Weather words
rainy · sunny · cloudy · windy · hot
Animals that live in water
fish · frog · turtle · whale · crocodile
Songs and Rhymes
The “Rainy Day” poem — say it together
Rain on the green grass.  /  Rain on the tree.  /  Rain on the houses  /  But not on me!
Wiggle your fingers like falling rain as you say it. Say it three times — a little louder each time!
“Row, Row, Row Your Boat” — sing and do the actions
Row, row, row your boat,  /  Gently down the stream.  /  Merrily, merrily, merrily, merrily,  /  Life is but a dream.
Sit facing your child, hold hands, and rock back and forth like rowing a boat.
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child in English: “Hello! How are you?”
Look outside together: “What is the weather like today? Is it sunny or rainy?”
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to real water (a cup, a tap, a puddle) and say the word clearly: “water”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 words at a time. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Water Hunt: ask “Where do we find water?” and let your child point (tap, cup, rain, a picture of the sea).
Sing the “Rainy Day” poem and “Row Your Boat”, doing the actions together.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “The Well in the Village”, aloud. Point to the words in bold and to any pictures your child has drawn, and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “How did we use water today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and remind them to say “Alhamdulillah” (thank you) for clean water.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I use water to ______.” (drink, wash, cook)
“All living things need ______.” (water)
“We find water in the ______.” (river, sea, well)
“It is ______ today.” (rainy, sunny, cloudy)
“The ______ floats.” / “The ______ sinks.”
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Says why we need water.   □
Names 3 places we find water (rain, river, sea, well).   □
Says one way they use water: “I use water to ______.”   □
Names 2 animals that live in water.   □
Joins in the “Rainy Day” poem or “Row Your Boat”.   □

paragraphs: Story  —  Unit 9: City Places
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  “A Walk Around Town”
A Walk Around Town
One sunny morning, Sagal held her mother's hand. “Today we will walk around our town,” said Mum. “Come and see!”
First they came to the big road. The traffic lights were red. “Red means stop,” said Sagal. They waited. Then the light was green. “Green means go!” said Sagal, and they walked across.
“Look, Mum! There is my school,” said Sagal. Her friend Yusuf waved from the gate. “Hello, Sagal!” he called. “Hello, Yusuf!” said Sagal. She smiled.
Next to the school was the market. It was very busy. A kind man was selling sweet mangoes. “Good morning,” said the shopkeeper, Abdi. “Would you like a mango?”
“Yes, please,” said Sagal politely. “Thank you!” Abdi smiled. “You are a polite girl,” he said.
They walked past the hospital. “The doctor, Hodan, works here,” said Mum. “She helps people when they are sick.” “Doctors are kind,” said Sagal.
Near the park, Sagal saw some litter on the ground. A little boy named Liban was dropping his paper. “Oh no,” said Sagal. “Please put your paper in the bin. We keep our town clean!”
“Sorry,” said Liban. He picked up the paper and put it in the bin. “Thank you for helping,” said Sagal. Now the park was clean and pretty.
Last of all, they came to the mosque. It was quiet and calm. “This is a special place,” said Mum softly. Sagal's grandmother, Amina, was there. “Come, my dear,” she said, and gave Sagal a warm hug.
On the way home, Mum asked, “What did you see in our town today?”
Sagal counted on her fingers. “I saw the school, the market, the hospital, the park, and the mosque! I saw kind people too.”
“And what did you learn?” asked Mum.
Sagal smiled a big smile. “Red means stop, green means go — and we all help to keep our town clean. I love my town!”
Let's Talk About the Story
Ask your child these questions. Any answer they try is a good answer!
What places did Sagal see in her town?
What does a red traffic light mean? What does green mean?
Who helps people when they are sick?
What did Sagal ask Liban to do with his litter?
What is your favourite place in YOUR town?

paragraphs: Unit 9: City Places
Year 1 English  ·  Ehel Academy  ·  Teacher & Parent Guide
What This Unit Is About

In this unit, your child learns the words for places in a town — the shop, the market, the school, the hospital, the mosque. They learn the people who work in these places, and simple words that tell us where things are (near, far, next to). They also meet the traffic lights and learn that red means stop and green means go. The big idea is that a town is full of helpful places and kind people, and that we all help keep our town clean and safe. The goal is warm, confident English — not perfect spelling.
What Your Child Will Be Able to Do
Name places in a town (shop, market, school, hospital, mosque, park).
Name some people who help us (teacher, doctor, shopkeeper).
Say where a place is using position words (near, next to, here, there).
Say what the traffic lights mean: red means stop, green means go.
Say “I go to the ______.” and “I can see a ______.”
Join in the traffic-lights rhyme and a town song.
Words We Will Learn
Places in a town
shop · market · school · hospital · mosque · park · road · bus stop
People who help us
teacher · doctor · shopkeeper · friend · neighbour
Where things are (position and direction)
here · there · near · far · next to · stop · go
Traffic lights
red (stop) · green (go) · yellow (wait)
Describing words
big · small · busy · quiet · clean · dirty
Songs and Rhymes
“The Traffic Lights” — say it together
Red means STOP.  /  Green means GO.  /  Yellow means WAIT,  /  Even if you're late.
Hold up a red thing, then a green thing. Freeze like a statue on “STOP” and march on the spot on “GO”. Say it three times — a little louder each time!
“I Live in a Town” — a happy song
I live in a town, yes I do,  /  I live in a town, yes I do,  /  Made by many kind hands,  /  All together built this town!
Clap along and name a place in your town after each line: “the market!”, “the school!”, “the mosque!”
How to Teach This Unit, Step by Step
Repeat this simple shape over several short sessions:
1. Warm-up (2–3 min)
Greet your child: “Hello! Where do we go today?”
Sing the traffic-lights rhyme with actions.
2. Learn the words (5 min)
Point to a picture of a place and say it clearly: “the market”. Ask your child to repeat.
Do 4–5 places at a time. Use big smiles and lots of praise.
Add a position word: “The shop is next to the school.” Point as you say it.
3. Play and sing (5 min)
Traffic Lights game: “Green!” — your child walks; “Red!” — your child freezes.
Town Walk: pretend to walk to a place. Ask “Where are we going?” Child says “To the ______!”
Sing “I Live in a Town” and name a place after each line.
4. Story time (5 min)
Read the Story file, “A Walk Around Town”, aloud. Point to the places and ask the simple talk-about questions.
5. Activity sheet (5 min)
Sit with your child and do one or two activities from the Child Activity Sheet. Read each instruction aloud for them.
6. Wrap-up (2 min)
Ask: “Where did we go today?” Celebrate! Say “Well done!” and one thing they did well.
Sentence Patterns to Say Out Loud
Speaking comes before writing. Practise these spoken patterns often:
“I go to the ______.” (school, market, mosque)
“I can see a ______.” (shop, park, bus)
“The ______ is next to the ______.”
“It is ______.” (big, small, busy, clean)
“Red means stop. Green means go.”
Simple Check — What to Look For
You do not need a test. Just watch and tick when your child can do these:
Points to and names 5 town places.   □
Names 2 people who help us (teacher, doctor…).   □
Says where a place is: “next to”, “near”.   □
Says what red and green traffic lights mean.   □
Says one thing: “I go to the ______.”   □

🧭  About this unit (please read first)
Your child is a beginner, about 5–7 years old, and is just starting to read. So YOU lead this unit — a teacher in class or a parent at home.
Work in short, happy sessions of about 15–20 minutes. Lots of pointing, saying, singing, colouring and playing — very little writing.
This guide tells you what to do. The Child Activity Sheet is what your child draws and colours. The Story is one you read aloud.
This is the LAST unit of Year 1. Take a moment at the end to celebrate everything your child has learned this year!

💬  Using the AI Tutor with a young child
The grown-up holds the device and reads with the child — do not leave a 5-year-old alone with it.
Ask the tutor to say a place word slowly so your child can copy the sound: “Please say the word ‘hospital’ slowly.”
Ask it to play a town game: “Ask my child where the doctor works.”
Ask it to sing the traffic-lights rhyme, or to give one more simple activity if your child wants to keep going.

🌟  Tips for teaching absolute beginners
Keep it short and playful. Stop while it is still fun.
Repeat, repeat, repeat — young children learn by hearing words many times.
Use your hands, face and voice. Point, act, and smile.
Praise every try. Confidence matters more than being correct at this stage.

🎉  Well done — your child has finished Year 1!
This is the last unit of Year 1. What a journey! Your child started as a brand-new beginner and can now name many English words, join in songs and rhymes, and say simple sentences out loud.
Take time to celebrate. Look back at their drawings from earlier units and let them show you what they can say.
In Year 2, your child will build on all of this — reading a little more, writing a little more, and speaking with more confidence. They are ready!
From all of us at Ehel Academy: well done to your child, and well done to YOU for teaching with love. 🌟
